findings.property: A capability token must only drive the session(s) and commands listed in its claims.
findings.title: REST control plane ignores token session/command scope
findings.attack: Mint a session-scoped token via the issuance flow (ClaimsFor binds Sessions=[A]). With that token, POST to /v1/sessions/<B>/input, /cancel, /permission or /model for any other live session B. Because the bound session is never checked, the holder drives session B — including answering permission prompts (auto-approving tool/file/command execution) on a session it was never granted.
findings.evidence: handle() calls `if _, err := s.verifyToken(r); err != nil` (rest.go:94) and DISCARDS the returned auth.Claims. handlePOST (rest.go:234) sets cmd.SessionID from the URL path and calls s.Mux.Dispatch with no claims in scope. Mux.Dispatch (multiplex.go:155-170) routes purely by cmd.SessionID. By contrast ws.go:99 correctly enforces `if !claims.AllowsSession(sess)`. claims.AllowsCommand is never consulted on the REST path either. ClaimsFor (issuance.go:176) proves tokens are intended to be session-scoped, and auth_test.go:109 only unit-tests AllowsSession/AllowsCommand in isolation — never the REST enforcement.
findings.proposedFix: Have handle()/verifyToken return the Claims to the inner handler (e.g. stash on request context). In handleSessionItem/handlePOST, after parsing sessID, reject with 403 when !claims.AllowsSession(sessID); for each verb also check claims.AllowsCommand(commandKind) before Dispatch — mirroring ws.go.

verdict.reason: Confirmed, could not refute. rest.go:94 discards the auth.Claims returned by verifyToken; handleSessionItem (rest.go:187-230) parses sessID purely from the URL and handlePOST (rest.go:234-282) sets cmd.SessionID=sessID and dispatches via Mux with no AllowsSession/AllowsCommand check. The WS path enforces exactly this at ws.go:99 (403 on !claims.AllowsSession(sess)), proving the REST omission is a real asymmetry. AllowsSession/AllowsCommand (auth.go:45,60) and ClaimsFor binding Sessions=[session] (issuance.go:176) confirm session-scoped tokens are an intended, issuable artifact — so a holder of a token scoped to session A can POST /v1/sessions/B/permission and auto-approve tool/file/command execution on session B. No rest/*_security_test.go exists; auth_test.go:109 only unit-tests the predicates in isolation and rest_test.go uses any-session (Sessions:nil) tokens, so the enforcement property is unasserted. Fixing it mirrors the documented scoping contract and contradicts no escape hatch.

findings.property: A session still in the PendingTwoFactor (pre-step-up) state must not be able to mutate or remove the user's second-factor configuration.
findings.title: Pending-2FA session can disable/reset 2FA, bypassing step-up
findings.attack: Attacker who knows a victim's password (phished/reused/breach) but not the TOTP logs in via POST /auth/login. Because the victim has 2FA enabled, CorePlugin.markPendingIfTwoFactorEnabled marks the new session PendingTwoFactor=true and sets the cookie. /auth/me correctly refuses this pending cookie, BUT the attacker instead sends POST /auth/2fa/disable with the same pending session cookie. getSessionUser only calls SessionStore().Get and returns sess.UserID without ever checking sess.PendingTwoFactor, so DeleteTwoFA removes the victim's 2FA. The attacker then re-logs in (HasTwoFactorEnabled now returns false) and gets a fully-authenticated, non-pending session — full account takeover with no knowledge of the second factor. The same pending cookie also reaches /auth/2fa/enroll, which overwrites the existing TwoFAState with Enabled:false (disabling the live secret) and lets the attacker re-enroll a secret they control.
findings.evidence: getSessionUser (twofa.go:224-235) does `sess, err := p.mgr.SessionStore().Get(...)` then `return sess.UserID` with no check of sess.PendingTwoFactor. disableHandler (twofa.go:468-482) and enrollHandler (twofa.go:242-277) and backupCodesHandler (twofa.go:486) gate ONLY on getSessionUser succeeding. Contrast meHandler (core.go:243) which explicitly does `if sess.PendingTwoFactor { 403 }`, and SessionMiddleware (session_middleware.go:109) which degrades pending sessions to anonymous. enrollHandler additionally does SetTwoFA with `state := &TwoFAState{Enabled:false,...}` (twofa.go:252-258), clobbering an already-enabled secret. The pending-session-refused property is tested at /auth/me (TestPendingTwoFA_Me_Rejected, twofa_test.go:625) but NOT at any /2fa/* mutation endpoint.
findings.proposedFix: Make getSessionUser (or the individual handlers) fetch the full Session and refuse when sess.PendingTwoFactor is true — return 403 'two-factor verification required' exactly as meHandler does. Have getSessionUser return (userID string, pending bool, err error) or a *Session, and gate disableHandler/enrollHandler/backupCodesHandler on !pending. Additionally, enrollHandler should refuse to overwrite a state where Enabled==true without a fresh step-up, so re-enroll cannot silently disable a live factor.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code. getSessionUser (twofa.go:230-234) calls p.mgr.SessionStore().Get and returns sess.UserID with NO check of sess.PendingTwoFactor. disableHandler (468-482), enrollHandler (242-277, which writes TwoFAState{Enabled:false} clobbering a live secret), and backupCodesHandler (486-515) gate only on getSessionUser succeeding. A pending session is a valid, retrievable session (PendingTwoFactor is just a flag, not an invalidation — see MemorySessionStore.MarkPendingTwoFactor session.go:158 and EntitySessionStore:430). The defensive intent is explicit and contradicted: meHandler refuses pending (core.go:243 'if sess.PendingTwoFactor { 403 }') and SessionMiddleware degrades pending to anonymous (session_middleware.go:109), but the 2FA handlers re-read the cookie directly via getSessionUser, bypassing the middleware. So an attacker with only the victim's password mints a PendingTwoFactor session at /auth/login then POSTs /auth/2fa/disable to delete the victim's 2FA, enabling full account takeover. The only pending-session test is TestPendingTwoFA_Me_Rejected (twofa_test.go:625) which asserts the property at /auth/me only; grep of all *_security_test.go shows zero coverage of 2fa/disable, 2fa/enroll, or 2fa/backup-codes with a pending cookie. Not a documented escape hatch — the doc-comments mandate the opposite (pending 'only usable for /auth/2fa/challenge').

findings.symbol: GetOne / ListAll / CountAll / UpdateOne / DeleteOne / BatchUpdateMany / BatchDeleteMany
findings.property: An in-process call against an OwnerField entity from a context with no owner must fail closed, not silently drop the owner predicate and span all owners.
findings.title: In-proc owner read/update/delete fail open (no requireOwnerContext)
findings.attack: A host with an OwnerField entity calls repo/CrudHandler methods from a context that carries no owner id (cron job, seed/migration script, queue worker, admin tooling, or any goroutine that forgot to inject the owner). e.g. ch.ListAll(ctx, ListOptions{}) or ch.GetOne(ctx, otherUsersDocID, nil) or ch.UpdateOne(ctx, otherUsersDocID, body) / ch.DeleteOne(ctx, otherUsersDocID).
findings.evidence: Every in-process method in crud_api.go calls requireTenantContext (fail-closed for MultiTenant) but only CreateOne (line 43) and BatchCreateMany (line 201) call requireOwnerContext. GetOne(108), ListAll(155), CountAll(283), UpdateOne(68), DeleteOne(90), BatchUpdateMany(229), BatchDeleteMany(259) call only ApplyOwnerScope*/ApplyTenantScope. ApplyOwnerScope (framework/crud/owner.go:36) is `if id, ok := owner.Get(...); ok { qb.Where(field+" = $1", id) }` — when ok==false it adds NO predicate, so the query returns/mutates rows for ALL owners. The RequireOwner doc-comment (owner.go:127) explicitly states this is the danger and that the framework is supposed to 'refuse requests that can't produce an owner id'; the HTTP List/Get/Update/Delete handlers enforce it via RequireOwner (crud.go:249,415,503,548,594) but the in-process twins do not. The tenant analogue was hardened and pinned by TestCrud_MissingTenantContextRejects (crud_api_security_test.go:77) for exactly these methods; the owner analogue is only pinned for Create/BatchCreate (TestCrud_AnonymousOwnerCreateRejected, line 61).
findings.proposedFix: Add `if err := ch.requireOwnerContext(ctx); err != nil { return ... }` at the top of GetOne, ListAll, CountAll, UpdateOne, DeleteOne, BatchUpdateMany, BatchDeleteMany — mirroring the existing requireTenantContext calls and the Create path. Then extend TestCrud_MissingTenantContextRejects with an owner-entity variant asserting errOwnerRequired for each read/update/delete method.

verdict.reason: Verified by execution: an anonymous (no-owner) context calling ch.ListAll returned both alice's and bob's rows, ch.GetOne fetched another user's row, and ch.DeleteOne removed another user's row, against an OwnerField="user_id" entity. crud_api.go GetOne(108), ListAll(155), CountAll(283), UpdateOne(68), DeleteOne(90), BatchUpdateMany(229), BatchDeleteMany(259) call only requireTenantContext; only CreateOne(43)/BatchCreateMany(201)/UpsertOne call requireOwnerContext. They rely on ApplyOwnerScope* which is fail-open (owner.go:41 adds the predicate only when owner.Get returns ok==true). The HTTP twins enforce RequireOwner (crud.go:249,415,503,548,594; crud_batch.go; crud_stream.go), and owner.go:127-131 documents this as the secure-by-default seam, so the fix aligns with the contract. The sibling crud_api_security_test.go pins owner fail-closed only for Create/BatchCreate (TestCrud_AnonymousOwnerCreateRejected) and pins read/update/delete only for the tenant axis (TestCrud_MissingTenantContextRejects on a non-owner entity) — the owner variant of those methods is unasserted.

findings.property: A session marked PendingTwoFactor must not reach any endpoint except /2fa/challenge.
findings.title: Pending-2FA session can fetch fresh backup codes, fully bypassing 2FA
findings.attack: Attacker who has stolen/guessed a victim's password (but not the 2nd factor) logs in. CorePlugin mints a PendingTwoFactor session (markPendingIfTwoFactorEnabled). With only that pending cookie the attacker sends GET /auth/2fa/backup-codes. getSessionUser returns the userID with NO PendingTwoFactor check, state.Enabled is true, so the handler GENERATES and RETURNS a fresh set of plaintext backup codes (also overwriting the victim's). The attacker then POSTs one returned code to /auth/2fa/challenge, which consumes it, calls markSessionTwoFA, and the same cookie is now fully authenticated. 2FA is defeated with the password alone.
findings.evidence: getSessionUser (twofa.go:224-235) only reads sess.UserID and never inspects sess.PendingTwoFactor. backupCodesHandler (twofa.go:486-515) calls getSessionUser then unconditionally regenerates and returns plaintext codes via SetTwoFA. challengeHandler.ConsumeBackupCode (twofa.go:380-390) accepts those codes. meHandler (core.go:243) DOES gate on sess.PendingTwoFactor, proving the pending gate exists but was omitted from the 2FA self-service handlers. The only pending-rejection test (twofa_test.go:625 TestPendingTwoFA_Me_Rejected) covers /auth/me, not /2fa/backup-codes.
findings.proposedFix: Add a pending-2FA gate to getSessionUser (or a dedicated requireNonPendingSession helper) that returns 403 when sess.PendingTwoFactor is true, and use it for enroll/verify/disable/backup-codes. Only challengeHandler should be reachable by a pending session.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code. getSessionUser (twofa.go:224-235) reads sess.UserID directly from the SessionStore and never inspects sess.PendingTwoFactor; it does NOT pass through the session middleware (session_middleware.go:109) that strips pending sessions, so that protection does not apply to the 2FA self-service handlers. backupCodesHandler (twofa.go:486-515) calls getSessionUser, gates only on state.Enabled (true for any pending-2FA user by definition), and regenerates plaintext backup codes overwriting the victim's. challengeHandler.ConsumeBackupCode (twofa.go:380-390) then accepts a returned code and calls markSessionTwoFA, fully authenticating the same cookie — defeating 2FA with the password alone. enroll/verify/disable share the same unguarded getSessionUser path. No *_security_test.go asserts pending-session rejection on these routes; the only pending test, TestPendingTwoFA_Me_Rejected (twofa_test.go:625), covers /auth/me, and TestTwoFA_BackupCodesRefresh uses a non-pending session. The documented invariant (session_middleware.go:107-108 and meHandler core.go:241 both state pending sessions are "usable ONLY for /auth/2fa/challenge") confirms the candidate's property — the fix aligns with the contract, not against it.

findings.property: Anchor href values must not carry script-executing URL schemes (javascript:/vbscript:/data:).
findings.title: Unsanitized Href enables javascript: scheme XSS
findings.attack: A nav tree / settings menu built from data (e.g. a user- or CMS-supplied resource link) where Item.Href = "javascript:alert(document.cookie)" renders <a class="nested-list__link" href="javascript:alert(document.cookie)">; clicking the link runs the script in the page origin. data: and vbscript: schemes behave identically.
findings.evidence: renderItem builds the leaf anchor with render.Tag("a", {"href": it.Href}, ...). render.Tag/Attr (core/render/html.go:60) only entity-escapes the value (&<>"'), which does NOT neutralize a scheme like `javascript:` — there are no special characters to escape. The framework already has a scheme sanitizer (framework/ui/safety.go safeURL, used by framework/ui/link.go, menu.go, components.go which drop javascript:/vbscript:/data:/protocol-relative/CRLF), but the core-ui/patterns builders bypass it entirely. core-ui/html does not sanitize schemes either (confirmed: no safeURL/javascript handling in core-ui/html/*.go). The identical flaw exists at tree/tree.go:121-125 (Node.Href) and breadcrumbs/breadcrumbs.go:91 (Crumb.Href). No sibling *_security_test.go exists in any patterns package and no existing test asserts href-scheme filtering (breadcrumbs_test only checks Text escaping at line 74).
findings.proposedFix: Add a shared scheme-allowlist sanitizer in core-ui (mirroring framework/ui/safety.go.safeURL: permit http/https/mailto/tel, relative/fragment/query paths; drop javascript:/vbscript:/data:/file:/blob:, protocol-relative //, and embedded/encoded CR/LF/NUL) and apply it to every Href before passing to render.Tag in nestedlist.renderItem, tree.renderNode, and breadcrumbs.renderCrumb. A dropped href should fall back to rendering a plain span (non-link) rather than an anchor with an empty href.

verdict.reason: Verified in core-ui/patterns/nestedlist/nestedlist.go:89-93: renderItem passes raw it.Href into render.Tag("a", {"href": it.Href}). render.Attr (core/render/html.go:60-65) only runs Escape, which replaces just &<>"'( html.go:26-33) — "javascript:alert(document.cookie)" has none of those chars, so it renders verbatim and executes on click. No scheme allowlist exists anywhere in core-ui (grep hits are runtime.js client-nav, not server HTML). The framework's own analogous builders DO sanitize (framework/ui/link.go:66 safeURL→fallback "#"; menu.go:177 sanitizeHref), proving href is treated as attacker-controllable in the framework's threat model, so this is not wrongLayer — and these "pure render" builders are explicitly for nav trees/settings menus fed from data. No *_security_test.go exists in any patterns pkg; nestedlist_test.go:38-44 only asserts a benign "/docs" href passes through, not scheme filtering. The safeURL doc-comment's escape hatch ("render the raw anchor via core-ui/html directly") is for core-ui/html, not for the intent-composing patterns builders, so a fix does not contradict it. Identical flaw confirmed at tree/tree.go:121-123 and breadcrumbs/breadcrumbs.go:91.

findings.title: Upsert ON CONFLICT hijacks rows of other owners/tenants
findings.property: An upsert must never let a caller mutate or take over a row owned by a different owner/tenant.
findings.attack: Authenticated user bob (owner_id=bob, or tenant B) calls UpsertOne with id set to an existing PK belonging to alice: {id: alices-post, title: pwned}. INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE matches alice's row purely by primary key and runs SET title=EXCLUDED.title, owner_id=EXCLUDED.owner_id, tenant_id=EXCLUDED.tenant_id, overwriting alice's data and re-stamping ownership/tenant to bob. No ownership/tenant check is performed against the pre-existing row.
findings.evidence: UpsertOne only calls requireOwnerContext (owner present in ctx) plus the MultiTenant-id-present guard; it strips+re-stamps owner_id/tenant_id from context (lines 55-60, 82-83). The soft-delete preflight (lines 67-81) is the only existence check and queries WHERE pk=$1 with NO owner/tenant scope. The conflict UPDATE SET is built from cols (which includes owner_id and tenant_id appended 128-133), emitting c = EXCLUDED.c for every non-PK/non-auto col (lines 137-146). Nothing constrains the ON CONFLICT target to rows the caller owns. upsert_security_test.go covers owner-stamp, body-cannot-override, anonymous-rejected, tenant-missing-rejected, but NOT cross-owner/cross-tenant takeover of an existing PK.
findings.proposedFix: In the inTx preflight, when a row with the body PK already exists, SELECT its owner_field/tenant_id and compare against context-derived owner/tenant; if they differ, return a forbidden/not-found error and abort instead of falling through to ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE. Apply the same owner/tenant scope to the soft-delete preflight SELECT so it cannot probe or clobber other principals' rows.

verdict.reason: Verified in framework/crud/crud_upsert.go. UpsertOne only guards owner-present (requireOwnerContext) and tenant-present (lines 41-51), then strips/re-stamps owner_id and tenant_id from context (lines 55-60, 82-83 via InjectOwner/InjectTenant). owner_id is a writable field so it enters `cols` and emits `owner_id = EXCLUDED.owner_id` in the SET clause (lines 108-146); tenant_id likewise (lines 128-133,145). The ON CONFLICT target is `(ch.PrimaryKey)` only (lines 162-164) with no owner/tenant in the conflict target or any WHERE. The single existence check is the soft-delete preflight (lines 67-81), which queries `WHERE pk=$1` unscoped and only inspects deleted_at. So bob upserting {id: alices-post} matches alice's row by PK and runs DO UPDATE SET title=..., owner_id=bob (EXCLUDED), tenant_id=B — overwriting alice's data and re-stamping ownership. No comparison of the pre-existing row's owner/tenant against the caller's context exists. Grepped upsert_security_test.go: tests cover owner-stamp on fresh insert, body-cannot-override (same caller), anonymous-rejected, tenant-missing-rejected, and soft-delete resurrection — none seed a row owned by a different principal then upsert under another principal, so the cross-owner/cross-tenant takeover property is NOT asserted. owner/tenant derive from authenticated context and the body PK is attacker-controlled, so this is the correct layer. The doc-comment (lines 24-33) only documents hook semantics; it makes no ownership-preservation promise, so a fix does not contradict it.

findings.property: Message body content must not be able to introduce new MIME parts or terminate the multipart container.
findings.title: MIME boundary injection via body content
findings.attack:
Send an email whose TextBody or HTMLBody (these are rendered from templates with attacker-influenced data, e.g. a user-supplied display name in a welcome/notification email) contains a line `
--gofastr-boundary-12345
Content-Type: text/html

<phish>...`. Because the boundary is a fixed constant and the body is written verbatim, this injects an attacker-controlled MIME part; `
--gofastr-boundary-12345--` truncates the message early.
findings.evidence: smtp.go line 206 hardcodes `boundary := "gofastr-boundary-12345"`, then lines 216 and 224 write `email.TextBody`/`email.HTMLBody` raw into the multipart stream with no scan for a line equal to the boundary delimiter. assertNoHeaderInjection (smtp.go:272) is only applied to From/Subject/To/Cc/Bcc/custom-header-keys/attachment filename+content-type (smtp.go:148-184) — never to TextBody/HTMLBody. The boundary is also predictable/constant, so the attacker knows exactly what string to embed.
findings.proposedFix: Generate a cryptographically random boundary per message (crypto/rand) instead of the fixed constant, and additionally reject (or quoted-printable/base64 encode) any body whose content contains a line beginning with `--`+boundary. Random per-message boundary alone defeats the predictable-injection case; combine with an explicit body-scan for defense in depth.

verdict.reason:
Confirmed in current code. smtp.go:206 hardcodes boundary := "gofastr-boundary-12345" and lines 216/224 write email.TextBody/HTMLBody verbatim into the multipart stream. buildMessage's assertNoHeaderInjection (smtp.go:272) is applied only to From/Subject/To/Cc/Bcc/custom-header keys+values/attachment Filename+ContentType (smtp.go:148-184) — never to TextBody or HTMLBody. Bodies are attacker-influenceable: template.go executeText uses text/template (no escaping at all), so a user-supplied value (e.g. display name) rendered into a .txt body containing "
--gofastr-boundary-12345
Content-Type:...

<evil>" or "
--gofastr-boundary-12345--" injects a new MIME part or truncates the message; the boundary is a fixed, predictable constant so the exact delimiter is known. CR/LF and "-" are not escaped even by html/template, so the HTMLBody path is also affected. The multipart branch requires hasAttachments || (TextBody!="" && HTMLBody!="") (smtp.go:202-205), a common realistic configuration. Grepped email_security_test.go: it asserts header injection for headers/recipients/attachment metadata and BCC/secret/reset-link log leakage, but contains NO assertion that body content cannot inject/terminate a MIME boundary — this exact property is uncovered. Not developer config (end-user data via template), no documented escape hatch contradicted. P1 is appropriate; the multipart-branch precondition is the only mitigating factor, not a refutation.

findings.property: A response carrying Vary: * must never be reused for any request (RFC 9111 §4.1).
findings.title: Vary: * responses are cached and replayed cross-user
findings.attack: Origin serves a personalized/auth-dependent GET response (no Authorization/Cookie request header, e.g. user identified via X-User, X-Forwarded-User, or a custom session header) with `Vary: *`. Request 1 (alice) is stored; request 2 (bob, same path/query) is served alice's body with X-Cache: HIT.
findings.evidence: parseVary() canonicalises each token via http.CanonicalHeaderKey; for the token "*" this returns "*" (verified) which is non-empty and so is NOT filtered out, despite the doc-comment claiming `*` is "handled separately" — there is no separate handling anywhere in the file. captureVariant then stores "*:"+r.Header.Get("*") = "*:" (empty, since no header named "*" exists), and variantMatches compares r.Header.Get("*") == "" which is always true. Result: the Vary:* entry matches every subsequent request. I confirmed this with a runnable test: rec2 (bob) received "personalized-1-for-alice" with X-Cache=HIT. None of the existing Vary tests in cache_security_test.go cover `*` — they only cover specific named headers (Authorization, Cookie, Accept-Language, Origin).
findings.proposedFix: Treat `Vary: *` as uncacheable: in isStoreable() (or before storing), if the response Vary header, when split, contains a `*` token, return false so the response is never written to the cache. Also fix parseVary to drop the `*` token as its doc-comment already promises, and guard reads so a stored entry that somehow carries `*` is never served.

verdict.reason: Confirmed by repro test. parseVary (middleware.go:163-180) canonicalises "*" via http.CanonicalHeaderKey which returns "*" — non-empty, so it is NOT filtered, contradicting the function's own doc-comment that "*" is "handled separately" (there is no such handling). captureVariant (line 184-193) then stores ["*:"+r.Header.Get("*")] = ["*:"] (empty, no header named "*"), and variantMatches (line 197-209) compares r.Header.Get("*")=="" which is true for every request, so the entry matches all subsequent requests. isStoreable (line 121-138) has no Vary:* check, so the response is stored. My test showed bob receiving alice's "personalized-1-for-alice" body with X-Cache=HIT when the origin emits Vary:* and users are distinguished by a non-credential header (X-User). The sibling cache_security_test.go covers Vary on named headers (Authorization, Cookie, Accept-Language, Origin) but never "*". The proposed fix (treat Vary:* as uncacheable) aligns with the doc-comment's stated intent rather than contradicting it. Not wrong-layer: the attacker-relevant distinguisher is a request header, and Vary:* is the origin's standard RFC 9111 §4.1 uncacheable signal the middleware is supposed to honor.

findings.property: Attacker-controlled query parameters must not drive unbounded memory allocation.
findings.title: Unbounded K in /query causes OOM allocation DoS
findings.attack: Authenticated caller POSTs a tiny JSON body to /embed/query: {"text":"a","k":1000000000}. The 1 MiB body cap does not help because the payload is a few bytes.
findings.evidence: index.go:256-273 reads k:=q.K with only a lower-bound guard (k<=0 -> 10); there is NO upper bound. k flows into candidateWidth(k) (index.go:244-250) which returns k*4, then into store.Candidates(ctx, qv, filter, width). FlatStore.Candidates (store_flat.go:109) does `hits := make([]Hit, 0, top*2)` where top=width=k*4, i.e. it pre-allocates a slice with capacity k*8 of Hit (Hit embeds a Chunk with strings/slices/maps, ~100+ bytes each). With k=1e8 this is make([]Hit,0,8e8) ~ tens of GB, causing immediate OOM/process abort. routes_security_test.go bounds body size but never bounds k; grep of routes.go/index.go/store_flat.go confirms no maxK exists (only the k<=0 lower guard and the unrelated chunker MaxRunes).
findings.proposedFix: Clamp k to a sane maximum at the entry point of Query (e.g. `if k > maxK { k = maxK }` with maxK ~1000) before computing candidateWidth, and/or clamp the `top` capacity hint in FlatStore.Candidates to len(s.chunks) (you cannot return more candidates than exist) so the make() capacity is bounded by the corpus size rather than caller input.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code. index.go:256-259 guards only the lower bound (k<=0→10); there is no upper clamp on the attacker-supplied q.K (Query.K is a JSON field at embed.go:57, decoded directly from the request body in queryHandler, routes.go:142). k flows into candidateWidth (index.go:244-250: w := k*4) and then store.Candidates(...,width) at index.go:273. FlatStore.Candidates does make([]Hit, 0, top*2) at store_flat.go:109 with top=width, so a tiny body {"text":"a","k":100000000} yields a capacity-hint allocation of ~8e8 Hit structs (~120 bytes each → tens of GB), aborting the process. The 1 MiB body cap (routes.go:14) does not help since the payload is a few bytes. grep confirms q.K is referenced only at index.go:256 with no maxK anywhere in the package. routes_security_test.go bounds body size (TestHTTPQuery_RejectsOversizedBody) and other properties but never bounds k, so this exact property is not asserted. k is genuine attacker input (request JSON), not developer config, and no doc-comment sanctions unbounded k (zero-value doc treats K as a small result count), so fixing it via a clamp contradicts no escape hatch.

findings.property: A numeric Min/Max bound on a Decimal field must reject values outside the bound.
findings.title: Decimal accepts "NaN", bypassing all Min/Max bounds
findings.attack: POST /<entity> with a Decimal field that has Min:0 (e.g. a non-negative amount), body {"amount":"NaN"} (or "nan"). schema.ValidateAll is invoked on the raw JSON body in framework/crud/crud_ops.go:42; validateDecimal calls strconv.ParseFloat("NaN",64) which succeeds and returns a NaN float, and every comparison `n < *f.Min` / `n > *f.Max` is false for NaN, so validation passes. The string "NaN" is then stored into the numeric/decimal column.
findings.evidence: validate.go:125 `n, err := strconv.ParseFloat(s, 64)` accepts "NaN"/"nan" (Go's ParseFloat recognizes these). Lines 129-134 guard with `n < *f.Min` and `n > *f.Max`; IEEE-754 makes both comparisons false for NaN, so the function returns nil. Verified at runtime: validateField(Decimal,Min:0, "NaN") and "nan" both return nil; the bound is fully bypassed. "Inf" happens to be caught only because +Inf>Max, not by design. No sibling *_security_test.go exists and validate_test.go has no NaN/Inf coverage.
findings.proposedFix: After ParseFloat, reject non-finite values: `if math.IsNaN(n) || math.IsInf(n,0) { return fmt.Errorf("must be a finite decimal number") }` before the Min/Max checks. Apply the same guard in validateFloat for the toFloat64 path.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in core/schema/validate.go:125 — strconv.ParseFloat("NaN"/"nan",64) returns err=nil with n=NaN (verified at runtime). The Min/Max guards at lines 129-134 use `n < *f.Min` and `n > *f.Max`, both of which are false for NaN per IEEE-754 (verified: n<0=false, n>100=false), so validateDecimal returns nil and the bound is fully bypassed. The body is the raw JSON request body (attacker input), validated via schema.ValidateAll in framework/crud/crud_ops.go and then body[f.Name] is written to the column. No *_security_test.go exists in core/schema/ and validate_test.go has no NaN/Inf coverage. Not developer config, no documented escape hatch. The same toFloat64 path in validateFloat does not accept string NaN (toFloat64 has no string case), so the Float bypass is only via a genuine float64(NaN), but the Decimal string path is plainly triggerable.

findings.property: A markdown link/image href must never resolve to a script-executing scheme (javascript:, data:text/html, etc.) as the browser parses it.
findings.title: javascript: URL bypass via interior tab/newline/NUL in scheme
findings.attack: Markdown source `[x](java\tscript:alert(1))` (a literal TAB, LF, CR or NUL embedded inside the scheme name; same with images `![x](java\tscript:...)`). The HTML5 URL parser strips tab/LF/CR/NUL from within a scheme before matching, so the rendered `href="java\tscript:alert(1)"` executes on click.
findings.evidence: isDangerousURLScheme only strips LEADING whitespace/control bytes (the for-loop trims from index 0), then does HasPrefix(lower, "javascript:"). An interior control char (e.g. between 'java' and 'script') makes the prefix check fail, so safeLinkURL/safeImageURL return the URL unchanged. parseLink uses strings.TrimSpace which also only trims leading/trailing, leaving interior control chars intact. Confirmed by execution: input `[x](java\tscript:alert(1))` renders `<a href="java\tscript:alert(1">`. The function's own doc-comment claims it 'matches the parser's view' by stripping control chars, but it only strips leading ones — so this violates the stated contract rather than a documented escape hatch.
findings.proposedFix: Before scheme matching, remove ALL tab (0x09), LF (0x0a), CR (0x0d) and NUL (0x00) bytes from the scheme portion (mirror what browsers do): build a normalized string with these bytes deleted and run the HasPrefix checks against that. Apply the same normalization (or reject the URL) inside safeLinkURL/safeImageURL so the stored href cannot contain them either.

verdict.reason: Confirmed by execution: RenderHTML("[x](java\tscript:alert(1))") emits `<a href="java\tscript:alert(1">` (interior tab byte preserved), and the same for `\n`, `\r`, `\x00`, and image src. In inline.go:279-298 isDangerousURLScheme only strips LEADING control bytes (the for-loop trims trimmed[0]), then HasPrefix(lower,"javascript:") — an interior 0x09/0x0A/0x0D/0x00 defeats the prefix match so safeLinkURL/safeImageURL pass the URL through unchanged. escapeAttr (htmlEscaper at inline.go:229-237) only escapes & < > " ' and does not strip these control bytes, so they survive into the href. Browsers strip ASCII tab/LF/CR from a URL before scheme resolution per HTML5 URL parsing, so java\tscript: resolves to javascript: and runs on click — a classic stored-XSS vector. The existing xss_security_test.go only asserts the plain javascript:/data:text/html forms (lines 11,21,120,142), never the interior-control-char variant. The doc-comment at inline.go:276-278 claims it strips control chars to "match the parser's view," so the fix restores the stated contract rather than contradicting an escape hatch.

findings.property: A typed Before-hook that forces a field to a zero/falsy value must have that mutation persisted to the row.
findings.title: Typed Before-hook zero-value mutations silently dropped by omitempty merge-back
findings.attack: Host registers a defensive OnBeforeUpdate("accounts", fn) where fn sets value.IsAdmin = false (or balance=0, approved=false, role="") to override attacker input. Generated struct field is `IsAdmin bool `json:"isAdmin,omitempty"``. Attacker PATCHes {"isAdmin": true}. Hook runs and sets v.IsAdmin=false, but mergeStructIntoMap marshals the struct with omitempty, which omits the false value, so it is never written back into the body map. The body still carries the attacker's is_admin=true, and doUpdate's SET loop writes is_admin=true. The defensive hook is silently a no-op for any zero/falsy target value -> privilege escalation / authorization bypass.
findings.evidence: mergeStructIntoMap does `b,_ := json.Marshal(src)` then unmarshals into `fresh` and copies `for k,v := range snake { dest[k]=v }` — it is additive only and relies on json.Marshal output. The codegen at cmd/gofastr/generate.go:706 emits every non-id field with `,omitempty`, so any field the hook sets to its zero value (false, 0, "") is dropped from the marshaled JSON and never overrides the client-supplied value already in `dest`. doUpdate (framework/crud/crud_ops.go:155-160) then SETs whatever remains in body. The existing tests only assert non-zero mutations flow back (TestTypedHook_BeforeCreate_MutationFlowsBack sets Title to a non-empty string) and use a test struct (typed_hooks_test.go:16) whose shape differs from real codegen; no test covers a hook forcing a falsy value.
findings.proposedFix: Do not rely on omitempty round-trip to reflect Before-hook mutations. Either (a) marshal the struct without omitempty for the merge step (use a reflection-based field copy, or a json encoder over a no-omitempty shadow), or (b) diff the pre-hook struct against the post-hook struct and apply only changed fields (including changes to zero), explicitly writing zero values into dest. At minimum, document that codegen must NOT emit omitempty on hook-merged fields, and have generate.go drop omitempty so json.Marshal always includes the field.

verdict.reason: Confirmed, not refuted. mergeStructIntoMap (framework/typed_hooks.go:185-199) does `json.Marshal(src)` then copies only the keys present in the result into the existing body map (`for k,v := range snake { dest[k]=v }`). The codegen at cmd/gofastr/generate.go:706 emits every non-id field with `,omitempty`, so a hook setting a generated `IsAdmin bool` (or balance int/role string) to its zero value produces JSON that omits the field — the override never lands in dest. I reproduced this directly: with dest={is_admin:true, balance:999} and a struct forcing IsAdmin=false/Balance=0, after mergeStructIntoMap dest is unchanged ({is_admin:true, balance:999}). The body map fed to the hook is the same map doUpdate's SET loop reads (crud_ops.go:117 ExecuteHooks(...,body) and line 155 `val,ok := body[f.Name]; ub.Set(...)`), so an attacker's `is_admin=true` survives a defensive OnBeforeUpdate that tries to force it false — privilege escalation. Not alreadyCovered: typed_hooks_security_test.go only asserts panic recovery, and TestTypedHook_BeforeCreate_MutationFlowsBack uses a NON-empty Title, never exercising the falsy path. Not wrongLayer: the PATCH body is genuine attacker input and the hook is the developer's defensive control. Not a contract contradiction: the OnBeforeUpdate doc-comment unconditionally promises mutations are reflected back into the body; there is no documented zero-value exemption.

findings.property: Cross-table owner scope on eager-loaded relations must always be enforced regardless of attacker-supplied scoped filters.
findings.title: include scoped filter on owner field bypasses related-table owner scope
findings.attack: Alice owns post p1, which has comments from alice and bob. Alice requests GET /posts?include=comments(user_id=bob). The scoped filter user_id=bob is accepted (it is a declared field on the comments entity), so applyRelatedOwnerScope sees node.Filters already contains the OwnerField and returns early WITHOUT injecting `user_id = alice`. The include query becomes SELECT * FROM comments WHERE post_id IN (p1) AND user_id = 'bob', returning bob's private comment on alice's post.
findings.evidence: applyRelatedOwnerScope (include.go ~540-547) iterates node.Filters and `return`s as soon as `f.Field == ownerField`, intentionally letting a caller opt out by supplying their own predicate on the owner field. But node.Filters is populated from parseScopedFilters (include.go ~195-244) which is fed straight from the URL `include=rel(field=val)` query string; parseScopedFilters accepts any field that is declared on the target entity, and the OwnerField (e.g. user_id) is normally a declared field. The opt-out comment assumes the predicate comes from a trusted API layer, but on the HTTP List/cursor path it is attacker-controlled. TestInclude_RelatedTableOwnerScope only exercises `include=comments` with no scoped filter, so the bypass is untested.
findings.proposedFix: Do not treat an attacker-supplied scoped filter as an owner-scope opt-out on the request-driven path. Either (a) always AND the context-derived owner predicate even when a filter on the same field is present (so a forged user_id is intersected with the real owner and matches nothing), or (b) reject scoped filters that target the related entity's OwnerField at parse time in parseScopedFilters. Keep the opt-out only for in-process TypedQuery callers, not HTTP-derived nodes.

verdict.reason: Confirmed empirically with a PoC: alice (UserID=alice) calling GET /posts?include=comments(user_id=bob) via ch.List() returns {"body":"bob secret","user_id":"bob"} — an IDOR. applyRelatedOwnerScope (include.go:543-547) returns early when any node.Filters entry matches ownerField, skipping injection of the context-derived owner predicate. On the HTTP path node.Filters comes entirely from parseScopedFilters (include.go:98,228-230), which accepts any field declared on the target entity, and OwnerField (user_id) is a declared field on comments — so the attacker-supplied user_id=bob trips the opt-out. The sibling include_owner_security_test.go (TestInclude_RelatedTableOwnerScope) only exercises include=comments with NO scoped filter, so this exact bypass is untested. The doc-comment ("lets advanced callers opt out by passing their own filter at the API layer") describes a trusted in-process opt-out; it does not authorize attacker-controlled URL filters to disable cross-table owner scoping, so the proposed fix (always AND the context owner predicate on request-derived nodes, keep opt-out for TypedQuery) refines rather than contradicts the contract.

findings.property: Eager-loaded/included relations on a soft-delete entity must hide rows whose deleted_at is set, same as direct reads.
findings.title: include eager-load returns soft-deleted related rows
findings.attack: comments is a SoftDelete entity. A comment is soft-deleted (deleted_at set). Any caller requests GET /posts/p1?include=comments (or /posts?include=comments). The eager-load runs SELECT * FROM comments WHERE post_id IN (...) with no `deleted_at IS NULL` predicate, so the deleted comment is returned in the included payload, leaking content the soft-delete read filter is supposed to hide (and bypassing the retention/compliance story soft-delete exists for).
findings.evidence: Every include loader in eager_filtered.go (loadHasManyFiltered line ~76, loadBelongsToFiltered line ~171, loadManyToManyFiltered line ~237) builds `SELECT * FROM <target> WHERE <fk> IN (...) <scoped filters>` and never appends `deleted_at IS NULL`, even when the target entity has Config.SoftDelete=true. The direct List/Get path applies ch.ApplySoftDeleteFilter, but applyIncludeTree (include.go) only injects owner scope (applyRelatedOwnerScope), never a soft-delete predicate. No *_security_test.go in the package asserts soft-delete suppression through the include surface (grep for 'include.*deleted' returns nothing).
findings.proposedFix: In applyIncludeTree/recurseLoadOnRawRows, when node.Target.Config.SoftDelete is true, append a `deleted_at IS NULL` predicate (as a non-parameterised clause or a synthetic filter) to the loader's WHERE for that node, mirroring ApplySoftDeleteFilter. Honour any explicit ?withTrashed gate the rest of the framework uses before disabling it.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code. The three include loaders in framework/crud/eager_filtered.go (loadHasManyFiltered L76 `SELECT * FROM %s WHERE %s IN (...)%s`, loadBelongsToFiltered L171 `SELECT * FROM %s WHERE id IN (...)%s`, loadManyToManyFiltered L237) build their WHERE from only the parent IN-clause plus filterClause(node.Filters); no `deleted_at IS NULL` is ever appended. The orchestrator applyIncludeTree/recurseLoadOnRawRows (include.go L305-407) injects only applyRelatedOwnerScope (L532) and never inspects node.Target.Config.SoftDelete. The direct read paths do guard via ch.ApplySoftDeleteFilter (crud.go L152-158, called from crud.go L341/L449, crud_api.go, crud_stream.go, crud_cursor.go), so a soft-deleted related row hidden on a direct read is returned through ?include=. Grep of all *_test.go shows zero include+soft-delete coverage (only softdelete_security_test.go / softdelete_public_security_test.go cover the direct List/Get/stream/cursor surface), and include_owner_security_test.go exercises the include path but asserts owner-scope only. This is normal API input (attacker treated as caller, not config), and the fix does not contradict an escape hatch — the documented ?trashed=true gate (crud.go trashedAllowed L174) is not even honored here. P1: for a related entity with no OwnerField the deleted rows leak to any caller (same property as TestSoftDelete_PublicListDoesNotExposeDeletedRecords, but on the include surface).

findings.property: A rendered href/anchor must never resolve to a javascript:/vbscript: scheme regardless of embedded URL whitespace.
findings.title: LinkButton scheme guard bypassed by interior tab/newline in scheme
findings.attack: LinkButton(LinkButtonConfig{Label:"x", Href:"java\tscript:alert(1)"}) (a literal TAB, or \n/\r, between 'java' and 'script'). Same input to ui.Menu items via sanitizeHref. Reachable wherever an href is derived from user/CMS data.
findings.evidence: isUnsafeScheme only strips LEADING whitespace/control bytes (loop at lines 581-585) then does strings.HasPrefix(lower,"javascript:"). An interior control byte (java\tscript:) is not a prefix, so the guard returns false and LinkButton renders it. html.Link attribute-escaping does NOT strip the literal TAB (only <>&"'), so the output is href="java<TAB>script:alert(1)"; browsers strip tab/newline from URLs before scheme resolution and execute it as javascript:. Confirmed by running LinkButton — rendered: href="java\tscript:alert(1)". menu.go::sanitizeHref (line 226) has the identical leading-only TrimLeft deny-list and the same bypass. Sibling ui_link_form_security_test.go and components_test.go::TestLinkButtonRefusesUnsafeSchemes only test leading-whitespace ("  javascript:"), not interior control bytes.
findings.proposedFix: Make LinkButton (and Menu) use the allow-list safeURL from framework/ui/safety.go instead of the deny-list isUnsafeScheme, OR have isUnsafeScheme/sanitizeHref strip ALL interior ASCII control bytes (0x00-0x20, 0x7f) from the scheme token before the HasPrefix check, matching how browsers normalize the scheme.

verdict.reason: Verified by running the code. isUnsafeScheme (components.go:578-599) only strips LEADING control bytes (loop lines 582-585) then does strings.HasPrefix(lower,"javascript:"), so isUnsafeScheme("java\tscript:alert(1)")==false. LinkButton (line 534) therefore does NOT panic; html.Link → render.Tag → Attr → Escape, and render.Escape (core/render/html.go:26-33) only encodes & < > " ' — the raw TAB survives, producing href="java<TAB>script:alert(1)". Probe output: LinkButton rendered "<a ... href=\"java\tscript:alert(1)\" ...>". Browsers strip ASCII tab/newline from the URL before scheme resolution (WHATWG), so it executes as javascript:. menu.go::sanitizeHref (line 226-235) has the identical leading-only TrimLeft and returns the input unchanged for the same vector (confirmed: returns "java\tscript:alert(1)"). Existing TestLinkButtonRefusesUnsafeSchemes (components_test.go:255-262) and ui_link_form_security_test.go only test leading-whitespace ("  javascript:") and plain "javascript:", never interior control bytes — so the property is NOT already asserted. The guards' own doc-comments declare intent to neutralize javascript:/vbscript:; the documented escape hatch (render raw anchor via core-ui/html) is unaffected, so a fix contradicts no contract. Severity P1: stored/reflected XSS on any href sourced from CMS/user data.

findings.property: A handler that panics must not silently and permanently destroy worker capacity.
findings.title: Handler panic kills worker goroutine permanently (poison-message DoS)
findings.attack: Enqueue a job whose attacker-controlled Payload drives the registered handler into a panic (nil-map index, json.Unmarshal into a fixed-size array, slice OOB, type assertion). Each panicking job permanently kills one worker goroutine; repeat WithWorkers(N) times and the entire pool is dead. The same code in MemoryQueue.worker (memory.go:51) crashes the whole process.
findings.evidence: db.go:380 calls `h(ctx, job)` directly with no `recover()`; Start (db.go:334-347) spawns a fixed set of workers once and never respawns. memory.go:49-77 worker()->processJob() likewise invokes the handler with no recover, so a panic unwinds past wg.Done and crashes the process. grep confirms the only recover() in the package is the Close/Enqueue channel race in memory.go, not any handler path. Additionally in DBQueue the panicking job was already UPDATEd to status='claimed' (db.go:181/213) and is never Ack/Nack'd, so it is also leaked.
findings.proposedFix: Wrap the handler call in a deferred recover() that converts a panic into an error and routes it through Nack (so the job retries/dead-letters) rather than killing the goroutine; for DBQueue also re-launch a replacement worker (or have workerLoop self-restart) so the pool cannot be drained.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code, not refuted. db.go:380 calls `h(ctx, job)` with no deferred recover, and Start (db.go:336-341) spawns a fixed set of workers once via `go func(){ q.workerLoop(ctx); ... }()` with no recover wrapping the call and no respawn. memory.go:69 calls `handler(ctx, job)` the same way; the only recover() in the package (memory.go:86) is the Close/Enqueue closed-channel-send race, not the handler path. An attacker-controlled job.Payload (json.RawMessage) that drives a handler into a panic (unmarshal into fixed array, nil-map index, OOB) propagates out of the bare goroutine and crashes the whole process for both backends — at least as severe as claimed. In DBQueue the row was already UPDATEd to status='claimed' (db.go:181/213) before the handler ran and is never Ack/Nack'd, so it is also leaked. No *_security_test.go exists in battery/queue and no test asserts panic→Nack/recover. The Handler doc-comment (queue.go:24) only says 'Return a non-nil error to trigger a retry' — no documented escape hatch makes panics intentionally fatal, so the recover→Nack fix contradicts nothing.

findings.property: A response marked Vary: * (uncacheable, varies on unkeyed factors) must never be stored or replayed across requests.
findings.title: Vary:* responses are cached and replayed to all users
findings.attack: An origin handler returns a per-user response with header `Vary: *` (the RFC 9111 way to say 'this varies on factors not in the request — do not cache'). User A's anonymous-looking GET (no Authorization/Cookie, so hasCreds=false) is stored; every later GET to the same path+query is served the SAME cached body as a HIT regardless of who they are.
findings.evidence: parseVary (middleware.go:163) canonicalizes each token via http.CanonicalHeaderKey but does NOT drop `*` — its own doc comment claims it excludes `*` ('excluding "*", which is uncacheable but handled separately'), yet nothing handles it. isStoreable (middleware.go:121) never checks for Vary:*. captureVariant produces `*:`+r.Header.Get("*") = `*:` (empty), and variantMatches checks r.Header.Get("*")=="" which is always true, so the stored variant matches every request. Verified with a live test: two GETs to the same path with `Vary: *` returned body1==body2 and X-Cache=HIT on the second.
findings.proposedFix: In parseVary, skip the token when it equals `*`, and/or in isStoreable treat any response whose Vary contains `*` as non-storeable (return false). The doc comment already promises this behavior.

verdict.reason: Reproduced live: two anonymous GETs to /me where the origin sets `Vary: *` returned body1==body2 with X-Cache=HIT on the second. parseVary (middleware.go:163-180) canonicalizes "*" via http.CanonicalHeaderKey (which leaves it unchanged) and does NOT drop it, despite its own doc comment (line 161-162) promising it excludes "*" as "handled separately" — nothing handles it. isStoreable (line 121-138) has no Vary:* check. captureVariant yields "*:" (empty value) and variantMatches compares r.Header.Get("*")=="" which is always true, so the cached variant matches every request, replaying one user's body to all. The sibling cache_security_test.go asserts Vary on named headers (Authorization/Cookie/Accept-Language/Origin) but has no Vary:* case, so this exact property is not covered. The fix aligns with the existing doc comment, so it contradicts no escape hatch.

findings.property: A kill-switch flag with a safe-on default must NOT fail open (return the unsafe non-fallback value) on a transient store error.
findings.title: BoolDefault fails open on store error in its second fetch
findings.attack: Operator wires a kill switch as documented: `if flags.BoolDefault(ctx, "kill-payments", true) { block() }`. A transient DB error (connection blip, pool exhaustion, lock timeout) occurs while the request is in flight. BoolDefault's first Get happens to succeed and returns a defined flag, but the second fetch inside e.Bool errors.
findings.evidence: BoolDefault (flag.go:192-204) guards ONLY its own `e.store.Get` call against `err` and returns `fallback` on error. It then returns `e.Bool(ctx, key)`, which performs a SECOND, independent `e.store.Get` (flag.go:146-149). When that second Get errors, Bool returns `false` unconditionally — NOT the fallback. So for a kill switch with `fallback=true`, an error on the second fetch yields false and silently re-enables the protected path. This directly contradicts the doc-comment promise (flag.go:185-191): 'a typo or accidental delete must not silently re-enable the protected path' and 'BoolDefault returns the supplied fallback ... when ... a storage error.' It is also a TOCTOU double-read: the flag definition can change between the two Get calls. No test in polish_test.go/sql_test.go exercises a store that errors on the second fetch.
findings.proposedFix: Make BoolDefault evaluate the flag it already fetched instead of re-calling e.Bool. Factor the decision logic in Bool into an unexported `evaluate(ctx, *Flag) bool` that takes the already-loaded flag, and have both Bool and BoolDefault call it: BoolDefault does one Get, returns fallback on err or nil flag, else returns evaluate(ctx, f). This removes the second unguarded fetch and the TOCTOU window, and makes the fail-safe behavior actually hold.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in core/featureflag/flag.go. BoolDefault (lines 196-203) guards only its OWN store.Get against err, then returns e.Bool(ctx, key) (line 203), which performs a SECOND independent store.Get (line 146). When that second Get errors, Bool returns false unconditionally (lines 147-148), NOT the fallback. So BoolDefault(ctx, "kill-payments", true) yields false on a transient store error during the second fetch, fail-open re-enabling the protected path — contradicting the doc-comment at lines 186-187 ("must not silently re-enable the protected path"). The fix (factor an unexported evaluate(ctx, *Flag) shared by Bool and BoolDefault, doing one guarded Get) honors BoolDefault's own contract without contradicting Bool's documented fail-to-false behavior. No *_security_test.go exists in the package, and the only BoolDefault tests (polish_test.go:10-37) use MemoryStore, whose Get never errors (flag.go:307), so the error-on-second-fetch property is unasserted. Trigger is a transient infra error (edge input), not developer config — correct layer. P1 not P0 because it requires the specific BoolDefault+true-fallback usage coinciding with a store-error window.

findings.property: User-controlled fenced-code-block info strings must never break out of the class attribute into element context.
findings.title: Fence info-string XSS via %q-escaped class attribute
findings.attack: A markdown document (any untrusted markdown rendered to HTML — comment, wiki page, README preview) containing a fenced code block whose info string includes a double quote, e.g. ```"> body ``` renders to live HTML.
findings.evidence:
renderFence builds the open tag with `fmt.Fprintf(sb, " class=%q", "language-"+lang)` (parser.go ~line 151). `lang` is `strings.TrimSpace(strings.TrimPrefix(open, fence))` — fully attacker-controlled. Go's `%q` escapes a `"` as backslash-quote (`\"`) and does NOT escape `>`. In HTML the backslash is a literal char and the `"` terminates the attribute value, so output `
">` is parsed as `class="language-\"` followed by a live `` element. Confirmed by running Render: output was `
">body`. The sibling xss_security_test.go only asserts code-block CONTENT escaping (TestMarkdown_CodeBlockEscaped) and other surfaces use HTML-entity escaping (escapeHTML/escapeAttr); the info string is the only place that relies on `%q` for HTML-attribute safety, and no test guards it.
findings.proposedFix: Do not use `%q` for HTML attribute values. HTML-escape the lang via escapeAttr and emit with explicit quotes: `fmt.Fprintf(sb, " class=\"%s\"", escapeAttr("language-"+lang))`. Optionally also restrict lang to an alphanumeric/`-_` allow-list before use, mirroring isSafeAttrKey, since a code-fence language identifier never legitimately contains quotes or angle brackets.

verdict.reason:
Confirmed by execution. parser.go:151 emits `fmt.Fprintf(sb, " class=%q", "language-"+lang)` where lang (parser.go:134) is `strings.TrimSpace(strings.TrimPrefix(open, fence))` — fully attacker-controlled markdown fence info string. Go's %q escapes `"` as `\"` (literal backslash+quote in HTML) but leaves `>` unescaped, so input ```"> renders to `
">body` — a live img element. Render() returns the trusted render.HTML type, so any untrusted-markdown surface gets stored/reflected XSS. The sibling xss_security_test.go TestMarkdown_CodeBlockEscaped (line 41) only asserts code-block CONTENT escaping, never the info-string attribute, so it is uncovered. The escapeAttr doc-comment (inline.go:239) explicitly promises attribute values from user-controlled context are always escaped, which renderFence violates — fixing it upholds, not contradicts, the contract.

findings.property: A recovered panic must never leak its raw value to the HTTP response body.
findings.title: HandlerAdapter leaks raw panic value into 500 body
findings.attack: Any typed handler that panics with a value carrying sensitive data (e.g. a driver that panics with `pq: password authentication failed for user "admin"`, or `panic(err)` where err is a DB/filesystem error, or a runtime error exposing an internal path) returns that value verbatim to the client. Trigger: send a request to an endpoint whose handler panics; the client receives `{"error":{"code":500,"message":"internal server error: <raw panic value>"}}`.
findings.evidence: handler.go:21-23 does `err := Errorf(http.StatusInternalServerError, "internal server error: %v", rec); WriteError(w, err)`. Errorf builds an *Error, and WriteError renders an *Error's Message verbatim (handler.go:118-141). So the formatted panic value reaches the client body. This directly contradicts WriteError's own doc-comment (handler.go:104-117) which states it 'prevents accidental leaks of database errors, driver-specific strings ("pq: password authentication failed for user \"admin\"")'. I confirmed with a live test: a handler panicking with `pq: password authentication failed for user "admin" at /secret/path` produced body `{"error":{"code":500,"message":"internal server error: pq: password authentication failed for user \"admin\" at /secret/path"},...}`. The recovery path differs from the returned-error path, which IS scrubbed (asserted by TestRespond_ErrorNoStackLeak in bind_security_test.go:228).
findings.proposedFix: In the recover handler, do NOT format the panic value into the client-facing message. Emit a fixed generic message (`WriteError(w, &Error{Code:500, Message:"internal server error"})`) and log the panic value + stack server-side via slog instead, mirroring middleware.RecoveryFn. The raw `rec` must stay out of the response body.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code and reproduced empirically. handler.go:21 does Errorf(500, "internal server error: %v", rec), producing an *Error whose Message embeds the raw panic value; WriteError (handler.go:118-141) renders an *Error's Message verbatim, so panic(dbErr) leaks to the client body. My probe returned {"error":{"message":"internal server error: pq: password authentication failed for user \"admin\" at /secret/path"}}. The sibling TestRespond_ErrorNoStackLeak (bind_security_test.go:228) only covers the returned-error path (WriteError called with a non-*Error, which hits the scrubbing !ok branch); it never goes through HandlerAdapter's recover. TestPanicRecovery (handler_test.go:570) only positively asserts the body contains "internal server error" and would still pass with the leak. Fixing it aligns with WriteError's own doc-comment (handler.go:104-117) that promises to prevent leaks of "pq: password authentication failed" strings — no contract contradiction.

findings.property: ProcessFileField must reject all stored SVG/HTML content that can execute script in a downstream renderer.
findings.title: DOCTYPE-prefixed SVG bypasses content sniffer (stored XSS)
findings.attack: Upload a file whose body is `<!DOCTYPE svg PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD SVG 1.1//EN" "...svg11.dtd"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" onload="alert(1)"/>` via the CRUD multipart upload path (crud_upload.go saveFilePart -> ProcessFileField). Any filename/extension; client Content-Type is ignored.
findings.evidence: In rejectUnsafeContent the leading-token prefix list only matches `<svg`, `<?xml`, `<html`, `<!doctype html`, `<script`, `<iframe`. A body starting with `<!DOCTYPE svg ...>` matches none of these (it is `<!doctype svg`, not `<!doctype html`), and the interior `<svg>` is not the leading token. The final fallback trusts http.DetectContentType, which I verified returns `text/plain; charset=utf-8` for this input (not text/html or image/svg), so none of the fallback cases fire. I ran rejectUnsafeContent on this exact payload in a package test: it returned nil (rejected=false), while a leading-comment SVG variant is correctly rejected. The SVG is then stored and served, executing the onload handler when rendered as image/svg+xml or sniffed by a browser.
findings.proposedFix: After the existing leading-token checks, also detect dangerous tags anywhere in the sniff window: lowercase `trim` (capped at 512B) and reject if it contains `<svg`, `<script`, `<iframe`, or a `<!doctype` whose following non-space token is not a known-safe root. Simplest robust fix: also reject when the leading token is `<!doctype` followed by anything other than `html` AND the window contains `<svg`; or more conservatively, reject any sniff window that contains the substring `<svg` or `<script` regardless of position, since neither belongs in a non-active content field.

verdict.reason: Verified in framework/file/filefield.go:274-295. The leading-token prefix list only matches "<svg", "<?xml", "<html", "<!doctype html", "<script", "<iframe". A body of `<!DOCTYPE svg PUBLIC ...><svg ... onload="alert(1)"/>` lowercases to a leading token "<!doctype svg" which matches none of these (the interior "<svg" is not the leading token). I ran http.DetectContentType on this exact payload: it returns "text/plain; charset=utf-8", so none of the fallback cases (text/html, image/svg, text/xml, application/xml) fire either. I also ran rejectUnsafeContent directly against the real package code with this payload and it returned nil (accepted). ProcessFileField (filefield.go:191) calls rejectUnsafeContent on the raw body and stores the file when it returns nil, so the SVG is stored and served as active content — exactly the threat the function's own doc-comment (lines 241-246) says it exists to block. This is attacker-controlled upload body, not developer config. The only sibling security test, TestProcessFileField_RejectsSVGByDefault (filefield_process_security_test.go:38-45), asserts only a plain `<svg`-leading body, so the DOCTYPE-prefixed variant is not already covered. No documented escape hatch permits stored active SVG.

findings.property: A Gallery item's Src/HrefFn URL must not produce an executable javascript: anchor href.
findings.title: Gallery anchor href skips safeURL, allows javascript: XSS
findings.attack: Render a gallery whose items come from user/CMS data with GalleryItem.Src = "javascript:alert(document.cookie)" (or an HrefFn returning the same). The default click branch and the HrefFn branch both write linkAttrs["href"] = it.Src / cfg.HrefFn(...) with no scheme check. render.Tag -> writeAttrs -> render.Attr only HTML-escapes the value; "javascript:alert(1)" contains no escapable characters, so the output is <a href="javascript:alert(1)" target="_blank">. Clicking the thumbnail executes script.
findings.evidence: gallery.go lines 209, 216, 218 assign linkAttrs["href"] directly from it.Src / cfg.HrefFn without calling safeURL (the framework/ui/safety.go helper that the sibling Image (image.go:108) and Link (link.go:66) components DO use). Verified live: Gallery(GalleryConfig{Items:[]GalleryItem{{Src:"javascript:alert(1)",Alt:"x"}}}) renders `<a ... href="javascript:alert(1)" rel="noopener" target="_blank">`. render/html.go Attr() (line 60-65) only applies Escape() to the value, never validates the URL scheme. gallery_test.go has no scheme/href-sanitization assertion (only checks the safe default href="/full.jpg").
findings.proposedFix: Route every gallery anchor href through the existing safeURL helper before assigning: e.g. `if h := safeURL(it.Src); h != "" { linkAttrs["href"] = h }` and likewise `if h := safeURL(cfg.HrefFn(i, it)); h != "" { linkAttrs["href"] = h }`. When safeURL returns "", drop the href (and the target/rel) so the item renders as a non-navigating figure. Add a gallery_security_test asserting a javascript:/data: Src never reaches the rendered href.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code and via live render. gallery.go:209/216/218 assign linkAttrs["href"] directly from it.Src / cfg.HrefFn(i,it) with no safeURL call, and the thumbnail img src (line 224, thumb defaults to it.Src) is likewise unsanitized. render.Attr (core/render/html.go:60) only HTML-escapes, which does not neutralize "javascript:alert(1)". A test render of Gallery(GalleryConfig{Items:[]GalleryItem{{Src:"javascript:alert(document.cookie)",Alt:"x"}}}) emits href="javascript:alert(document.cookie)" (confirmed). The package's own safeURL helper (safety.go:22) drops javascript:/data:/vbscript:, and sibling components Link (link.go:66) and Image (image.go:108) route through it — Gallery is the inconsistent gap. None of the three sibling *_security_test.go files cover Gallery, so the property is not already asserted. safety.go's doc states UI builders are the enforcement layer ("UI builders no longer make that decision"), so fixing Gallery does not contradict any documented escape hatch. Attacker-reachable when gallery items come from user/CMS data.

findings.property: A response declaring Vary: * is per-request-unique and must never be reused, but the middleware caches it under a plain key and replays it to every client.
findings.title: Vary: * responses cached and replayed cross-user
findings.attack: An origin handler returns user-specific content with header `Vary: *` (the HTTP idiom for "this response varies on unstated dimensions, do not reuse"). User A's request populates the cache; User B's request to the same path+query gets a HIT and receives User A's response body verbatim.
findings.evidence: baseKey at line 57 omits any Vary dimension. parseVary (line 163) splits the Vary header and silently DROPS the "*" token (its doc comment at line 161 even claims "*" is "handled separately"), so for `Vary: *` it returns nil. isStoreable (line 121) never inspects the Vary header, so the response is stored. captureVariant(r, nil) returns nil, meaning the entry records NO variant constraints, and variantMatches over an empty list always returns true. I confirmed with a test: two identical GET /p requests to a handler emitting `Vary: *` returned X-Cache=HIT on the second with body "user-specific-1". The claim in the doc-comment that "*" is handled separately is false — flagging the contradiction.
findings.proposedFix: In isStoreable (or in CacheMiddleware before Set), detect the "*" token in the raw Vary response header (case-insensitive, after trimming) and refuse to store the response, exactly like Set-Cookie / Cache-Control: private. Update the parseVary doc comment to match.

verdict.reason: Confirmed by running the real code: two identical GET /p requests to a handler emitting `Vary: *` returned X-Cache=HIT with body "user-specific-1" on the second request — cross-request replay of user-specific content. `isStoreable` (line 121) never inspects Vary, so the response is stored. The finder's claimed line-level mechanism is partly wrong (`parseVary("*")` does NOT return nil — `http.CanonicalHeaderKey("*")` returns "*", so parseVary returns ["*"]), but the outcome is the same: `captureVariant(r, ["*"])` synthesizes the vacuous variant key "*:"+r.Header.Get("*") = "*:" (the request has no "*" header), and `variantMatches` (line 197) then checks r.Header.Get("*")=="" which is true for every client, so the constraint never excludes anyone. The doc-comment at lines 161-162 falsely claims "*" is "handled separately" — it is handled nowhere. No sibling test asserts `Vary: *` (cache_security_test.go covers only Authorization/Cookie/Accept-Language/Origin). `Vary: *` is a legitimate origin signal, not attacker config, so guarding it at isStoreable is the correct layer.

findings.property: A leaf node Href must never render a script-executing or origin-ambiguous URL scheme into an anchor href.
findings.title: tree (and breadcrumbs) Node.Href bypasses safeURL → javascript: XSS
findings.attack: A navigation/outline tree is built from data where a node's link target is (partly) user-controlled — e.g. a bookmark tree, a CMS folder, or an admin/user-built menu where the URL is a stored field. Set Node.Href = "javascript:alert(document.cookie)" (or "data:text/html,
findings.evidence: renderNode puts n.Href straight into the href attr (tree.go:121-125) with no scheme check; render.Tag/Attr (core/render/html.go:60-65) only HTML-escapes the value — javascript:/data:/vbscript: contain no chars that escaping neutralizes, so the live scheme survives. The framework already treats this exact field as attacker-reachable: core-ui/patterns/nestedlist/nestedlist.go:93 routes Item.Href through safeURL() and is covered by nestedlist_security_test.go::TestHrefSchemeAllowlist (asserts javascript/vbscript/data are dropped). framework/ui/safety.go::safeURL exists for the same purpose. tree and breadcrumbs simply never call it; neither package has a *_security_test.go and their _test.go files only test a benign "/" href.
findings.proposedFix: Add the same safeURL allow-list helper used in nestedlist.go (http/https/mailto/tel/relative/fragment only; drop control bytes, %0d/%0a, protocol-relative //, and all other schemes). In tree.renderNode gate the anchor: if href := safeURL(n.Href); href != "" render <a href=href> else fall back to the <span> label. Apply the identical guard to breadcrumbs.renderCrumb (breadcrumbs.go:91). Add a tree/breadcrumbs *_security_test.go mirroring nestedlist's TestHrefSchemeAllowlist.

verdict.reason: Confirmed: tree.go:121-125 renderNode and breadcrumbs.go:90-91 renderCrumb emit n.Href/c.Href straight into an anchor href with no scheme check; render.Escape (html.go:26-32) only replaces &<>"' so a javascript:/vbscript:/data: scheme survives intact, and render.Attr/Tag validate the key only, never the value. The sibling pattern nestedlist.go:88-101 routes the identical Href field through safeURL() (lines 118-165, dropping javascript/vbscript/data/protocol-relative/control bytes) and asserts it in nestedlist_security_test.go::TestHrefSchemeAllowlist — establishing the codebase's own contract that this stored-data field is attacker-reachable. Neither tree nor breadcrumbs has any *_security_test.go; their tests only exercise a benign "/" href, so this property is not already covered. The safeURL doc-comment even states the patterns builders bypass the framework layer and must enforce the allow-list themselves, so the fix aligns with the documented contract rather than contradicting it. This is a genuine stored/reflected XSS sink, not developer-config input.

findings.property: The WebFetch tool must not reach private/link-local addresses or follow redirects into them.
findings.title: WebFetch has no SSRF guard, reaches metadata/internal IPs
findings.attack: A model-chosen or prompt-injected URL like http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ or http://127.0.0.1:<port>/ is fetched and the body returned to the model and session log. A vetted public URL can also 302-redirect to an internal address since the default client follows redirects with no re-validation.
findings.evidence: Run validates only u.Scheme (lines 85-89); the default http.Client (lines 91-94) has no CheckRedirect and no destination-IP filtering, and the resolved host is never checked against private ranges. WebFetch{} is wired with nil HTTPClient in source.go line 29. webfetch_test.go has no SSRF/redirect test. The threat-model doc lists scheme/header/size guards but never private-network protection.
findings.proposedFix: Resolve the host and reject loopback/link-local/private/unspecified IPs before the request; set client.CheckRedirect to re-run that validation on every hop and cap redirects.

verdict.reason: Verified in webfetch.go: Run validates only u.Scheme (lines 85-89) then builds a request with the default http.Client (lines 91-94) which has no CheckRedirect and no destination-IP filtering; the resolved host is never checked against loopback/link-local/private/unspecified ranges. source.go:29 wires WebFetch{} with nil HTTPClient, so production gets the redirect-following default client. A model-chosen or prompt-injected URL like http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ or http://127.0.0.1:<port>/ is fetched and the body surfaced to the model and session log; a vetted public URL can also 302-redirect to an internal address with no re-validation. This is genuine attacker input: the threat model (harness-architecture.md:182-183) declares 'Web content fetched by the agent (WebFetch). Always untrusted.' No documented escape hatch covers it — the 'What this model does not defend against' section (lines 230-239) never lists SSRF/internal-network reachability, and the standing WebFetch rule (lines 225-226) only mentions header stripping. No *_security_test.go in framework/harness/tool/builtins asserts SSRF/redirect/private-IP protection (webfetch_test.go only covers header stripping, non-http scheme rejection, and 404).

findings.property: A related entity's Hidden fields must never appear in the response, even when reached through ?include=.
findings.title: Include eager-load leaks related entity Hidden fields
findings.attack: Declare a related entity with a Hidden secret column (e.g. users.password_hash Hidden:true) and a relation from posts->author. Request GET /posts?include=author (or any include path). The related user row is returned with password_hash included.
findings.evidence: All three include loaders run `SELECT * FROM <related>` (eager_filtered.go lines 91, 186, 253) and copy every returned column into the attached row map (e.g. lines 114-117). The top-level read path scrubs Hidden via ch.VisibleFields() (crud.go:194-198), but the include path never consults VisibleFields or the target entity's Hidden flags. applyIncludeTree/formatRelationValueDeep (include.go) only case-converts keys, no scrubbing. The sibling test TestInclude_SensitiveRelationNotExposed (deep_security_test.go:1030) is mislabeled: it places password_hash Hidden on the SAME base entity and requests /sr_items with NO include and NO relation, so it only exercises the base list scrub — the actual related-entity include leak is unasserted. mcp.go:65-68 documents Hidden as a security control ('value-disclosure oracle over a secret column').
findings.proposedFix: In the eager loaders, project only the target entity's visible columns instead of SELECT *. Plumb the resolved target *entity.Entity (node.Target) into loadIncludeNode and build the column list from target.VisibleFields()-equivalent (skip f.Hidden). For the no-registry flat fallback where target is nil, post-filter attached rows by stripping any column whose schema field is Hidden, or refuse Hidden columns by name from the relation's declared schema.

verdict.reason: Confirmed and empirically triggered. All three include loaders in eager_filtered.go use SELECT * (lines 91, 186, 253) and copy every column into the attached row (lines 114-117, 210-214, 285-291), never consulting the target entity's Hidden flags even though node.Target is available. The base read path scrubs Hidden by column projection (crud.go:300 projectFromRequest -> VisibleFields() at crud.go:195-198), but applyIncludeTree/formatRelationValueDeep/deepConvertMap (include.go) only case-convert keys, no Hidden filtering. The sibling test TestInclude_SensitiveRelationNotExposed (deep_security_test.go:1030) puts password_hash Hidden on the SAME base entity sr_items and requests /sr_items with NO include and NO relation, so it only asserts the base-list scrub; the related-entity include leak is unasserted. I wrote a posts->author ManyToOne test with rf_users.password_hash Hidden:true and a wired registry: GET /rf_posts?include=author returned {"author":{...,"password_hash":"super_secret_hash"}}. The trigger is attacker-controlled (?include=) and Hidden is a documented attacker-facing read control, so not wrong-layer and no escape-hatch contradiction.

findings.property: Caller-supplied head HTML must not deliver any tag that fires an event handler in the browser.
findings.title: Head scrubber misses input/select/textarea/keygen autofocus XSS
findings.attack: A screen returns per-page head HTML from CMS/per-record data via SEOScreen.HeadHTML() (or WithHeadHTML). Payload `<input autofocus onfocus=alert(document.cookie)>` (also `<select>`, `<textarea>`, `<keygen>` with autofocus+onfocus/oninput) passes stripInlineScripts untouched. Browsers hoist these form controls into <body> and fire onfocus via autofocus, executing attacker JS.
findings.evidence: dangerousHeadTagsRe (line 1970) enumerates a fixed tag list (iframe|object|style|svg|math|audio|video|form|button|picture|marquee|portal|template|noscript|foreignObject|details|summary) and voidHeadTagsRe lists base|embed|img|source. input, select, textarea, keygen are in NEITHER list, and stripInlineScripts has no generic on*-attribute strip — only tag removal. Verified with the exact production regexes: `<input type=hidden onfocus=alert(1) autofocus>`, `<select autofocus onfocus=alert(1)>`, `<textarea autofocus onfocus=alert(1)>`, `<keygen autofocus onfocus=alert(1)>` all pass through verbatim. The doc-comment at line 1997 explicitly claims allowed survivors are only <meta>(non-refresh), safe <link>, <title>, and text — so these survivors violate the stated contract.
findings.proposedFix: Add a generic event-handler strip pass to stripInlineScripts that removes any on*= attribute (and/or autofocus) from surviving tags, OR extend dangerousHeadTagsRe/voidHeadTagsRe to cover input|select|textarea|keygen. Prefer the generic on*-attribute scrub so future form/interactive tags are covered too.

verdict.reason: Verified in uihost.go: dangerousHeadTagsRe (line 1970) and voidHeadTagsRe (line 1978) omit input|select|textarea|keygen, and stripInlineScripts (line 1999) does only tag removal with no generic on*-attribute strip. Ran the exact production regexes against `<input autofocus onfocus=alert(1)>`, `<select ...>`, `<textarea ...>`, `<keygen ...>` — all pass through verbatim. The scrubbed string is appended raw into head parts (line 928, and ds.headHTML at line 1028) with no escaping, so the surviving form control lands unescaped in the document head and fires onfocus via autofocus after browser body-hoisting. The doc-comment at lines 1997-1998 says allowed survivors are only <meta>(non-refresh)/safe <link>/<title>/text, so these survivors violate the stated contract (no escape-hatch contradiction). The sibling seo_security_test.go dangerousHeadTags table (lines 46-69) tests svg/iframe/details/video/form etc. including unclosed openers, but covers NO form-control+autofocus vector, so this exact property is not asserted. The escape hatch is documented as intended for CMS/attacker-influenced metadata, so this is in-threat-model, not developer-input misuse.

findings.property: Inline markdown rendering must bound recursion depth so attacker-supplied source cannot exhaust the goroutine stack.
findings.title: Unbounded inline recursion (nested links/emphasis) → stack-exhaustion DoS
findings.attack: POST/render attacker-controlled markdown of the form strings.Repeat("[",n)+"x"+strings.Repeat("](u)",n) (a few hundred KB). parseLink matches the balanced brackets, and renderInline(text) at line 58 recurses once per nesting level. With ~200k levels the render goroutine recurses ~200k deep; the earlier-captured `go test` stack trace shows an unbroken chain of renderInline frames at inline.go:58 unwinding, and the process never completes within 60s / is killed. The same unbounded recursion exists for emphasis inner content (lines 83/87/91 via renderInline(inner)).
