findings.evidence: Handler calls r.ParseMultipartForm(cfg.MaxSize) at upload.go:49 but there is no defer r.MultipartForm.RemoveAll() anywhere in the function (grep of core/upload finds zero RemoveAll references). The Go net/http server does NOT auto-delete multipart temp files; cleanup of *os.File temp parts relies solely on a runtime finalizer that runs at an unspecified, GC-driven time — not deterministically per request. Every early-return validation failure (lines 63, 69, 75, 87) also leaves the spilled part on disk because RemoveAll is never invoked. No sibling test asserts temp-file cleanup (grep for RemoveAll/tempfile in *_test.go returns nothing).
findings.proposedFix: Immediately after a successful ParseMultipartForm, add `defer func(){ if r.MultipartForm != nil { _ = r.MultipartForm.RemoveAll() } }()` so spilled temp files are removed on every return path. Additionally wrap r.Body in http.MaxBytesReader to bound the total request size when cfg.MaxSize > 0.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code: core/upload/upload.go:49 calls r.ParseMultipartForm(cfg.MaxSize) where cfg.MaxSize is the maxMemory arg; Go's stdlib spills file parts exceeding maxMemory to os.CreateTemp files on disk (confirmed via go doc). A grep of core/upload finds zero RemoveAll references, and the handler never calls r.MultipartForm.RemoveAll() on any return path (lines 50,56,63,69,75,87,100). An attacker controls the multipart body and can force a spill by sending a 'file' part larger than cfg.MaxSize; the ext/MIME/size validation-reject paths abandon the already-spilled temp file. The property is not asserted by any *_security_test.go (the hardening test at upload_hardening_security_test.go:55-72 covers Storage-backend partial-write cleanup, a different concern). It is at the correct layer and contradicts no doc comment — in fact the same codebase applies the correct pattern at core/middleware/csrf.go:321 (defer form.RemoveAll()), proving the omission is a genuine gap. Refined to P2, not P1: Go's multipart package sets a runtime finalizer on the temp *os.File that reclaims it on GC, so this is a non-deterministic resource-pressure / FD window under sustained uploads, not a permanent unbounded leak or auth/data-disclosure bug; the title's 'never removed' overstates the impact. The proposed fix (defer RemoveAll, plus http.MaxBytesReader) is the correct standard Go remedy.

findings.property: Rendering markdown must complete in time/space bounded near-linearly in input size, with no super-linear blowup on attacker-controlled nesting.
findings.title: Quadratic blowup on nested blockquotes (CPU DoS)
findings.attack: Submit markdown whose lines are a long run of '> ' prefixes followed by text, e.g. "> > > > ... x" with N nesting levels (each level is just 2 bytes '> '). A ~80–100 KB body (~40–50k levels) burns multiple seconds of CPU per request. Any host app that renders user-submitted markdown (comment, bio, doc) via markdown.Render / RenderHTML is affected.
findings.evidence: renderBlockquote (parser.go:180-196) strips one '>' (plus a space) from every line of the current block, strings.Join's the remaining lines, and calls renderBody on the joined string. renderBody (markdown.go:40) re-runs splitLines over that whole string and dispatches back into renderBlockquote for the still-'>'-prefixed content. Each nesting level therefore re-scans and re-copies O(n) bytes, giving O(n^2) total work. Measured: 10k levels=169ms, 20k=647ms (4x for 2x input), 40k=2.75s (4x for 2x input) — clean quadratic. No depth or size limit exists, and no markdown test (markdown_test.go / xss_security_test.go) asserts a nesting bound. (The already-found emphasis quadratic is in findClosingDelim — a different surface/property.)
findings.proposedFix: Bound recursion depth and/or avoid the O(n) re-join per level. Simplest: thread a depth counter through renderBody/renderBlockquote and stop nesting (emit the remaining text verbatim, escaped) past a small cap (e.g. 16). Alternatively cap total input length in Render, or rewrite blockquote handling to strip all leading '>' prefixes iteratively rather than recursing through a full re-parse.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code and reproduced empirically. renderBlockquote (core/markdown/parser.go:180-196) strips one '>' (+ optional space) from each line, then calls renderBody on strings.Join(inner, "\n"); renderBody (markdown.go:40-72) re-runs splitLines over the whole remaining string and dispatches back into renderBlockquote for still-'>'-prefixed content. A single line of N '> ' prefixes recurses N levels, each re-scanning/re-copying O(n) bytes = O(n^2). Measured RenderHTML on strings.Repeat("> ", N)+"x": 10k levels (20KB)=176ms, 20k (40KB)=671ms, 40k (80KB)=3.1s — clean quadratic, ~3s CPU per ~80KB request. No input-length cap and no depth bound exist in the package (grep found none), and no test (markdown_test.go / xss_security_test.go) asserts a nesting/size bound; markdown_test.go:47 only asserts blockquotes recurse, not that they are bounded. Reachable via markdown.Render/RenderHTML on any user-submitted markdown (comment/bio/doc), so the input is genuinely attacker-controlled, not developer config. Distinct from the prior emphasis quadratic in findClosingDelim. P2 is appropriate: CPU-DoS amplification, not RCE/data exposure.

findings.title: data:image/svg+xml allowed by signal URL guard
findings.property: The signal URL-scheme guard must reject every scriptable value before it is written into a url-bearing attribute.
findings.attack: An attacker controlling a signal value via a query-string deep-link param (the documented trust boundary in this function's doc-comment) supplies value=data:image/svg+xml,<svg onload=alert(document.cookie)/>. setSignal attr-mode calls _isUnsafeSignalUrl('src', value); the data: branch returns false (treated SAFE) and the value is written via setAttribute('src',v). When the bound element is an <iframe>/<object> whose src loads the data as a document, the inline SVG script executes.
findings.evidence: Lines 570-574: for data: it returns !trimmed.startsWith('data:image/'), so data:image/svg+xml passes the allowlist. SVG is a scriptable document format; the allowlist comment justifies data:image/* for inline blob previews, but the guard is applied uniformly to src/xlink:href/formaction on ANY element via setSignal attr-mode (line 851) and navigate() (line 601). Sibling runtime_security_test.go only asserts javascript:/vbscript: interior-control stripping; SVG data URIs are untested.
findings.proposedFix: Tighten the data: allowlist to non-scriptable image subtypes: reject data:image/svg explicitly, e.g. return !(trimmed.startsWith('data:image/') && !trimmed.startsWith('data:image/svg')). Add a security-test case for data:image/svg+xml returning unsafe.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in code: runtime.js line 573 returns `!trimmed.startsWith('data:image/')`, so `data:image/svg+xml,<svg onload=...>` yields false (SAFE) and is written via setAttribute at line 852 in setSignal attr-mode. The attacker trust boundary is real and documented: src/widgets.js:41-42 reads `url.searchParams.get(k)` for declared deep-link params and calls `NS.setSignal(k, v)` with the raw query value, exactly matching the doc-comment at lines 552-554. SVG is a scriptable document format that executes inline script when its data URI loads as a document (iframe src / object / embed), and the guard is applied uniformly with no element-type restriction (grep found no iframe/tagName gating). The guard's own doc-comment (line 549) states it "Rejects ... other data: payloads," so blocking SVG aligns with — does not contradict — the contract; SVG is the one scriptable subtype that slips the data:image/ allowlist. The sibling runtime_security_test.go only asserts the interior-control-char strip regex (TestSchemeGuardStripsInteriorControls), never data:image/svg+xml. Severity P2 (not higher) because the exploit needs a developer to bind a deep-linkable signal to a document-loading attr (iframe/object src), not the default img blob-preview case (img context does not run SVG script); the navigate()/href path is not viable since pushState to a data: URL throws SecurityError.

findings.property: The credential-exfil bash blocklist must block named commands regardless of invocation form.
findings.title: Bash blocklist bypassed by absolute path or prefix
findings.attack: The model runs /usr/bin/security dump-keychain, or `true; security ...`, or `command secret-tool ...`; leadingCommand returns the first token (`/usr/bin/security` or `true`), so the exact-string blocklist never fires and the blocked credential tool executes.
findings.evidence: leadingCommand strips only leading ` \t\n;|&(` then returns the first token (lines 152-162); match is `leading == banned` exact-string (lines 85-91). Absolute-path, env-prefix, or separator-after-benign-token all defeat it. Comment frames it as defense in depth (lines 21-23), but the basename/path mismatch defeats the blocklist for a single direct absolute-path invocation.
findings.proposedFix: Normalize the leading token via filepath.Base before comparison and scan the leading token of each pipeline/separator-split segment, keeping the permission middleware as the authoritative gate.

verdict.reason: I tried to refute but the bypass is real and triggerable in current code. `leadingCommand` (bash.go:152-162) only does `strings.TrimLeft(cmd, " \t\n;|&(")` then returns the first whitespace-delimited token; it never normalizes via filepath.Base nor splits on `;`/`|`/`&&` inside the string. The blocklist match is exact-string `leading == banned` (bash.go:86). So `/usr/bin/security dump-keychain` -> "/usr/bin/security" != "security"; `true; security ...` -> "true;" != "security"; `command secret-tool ...` -> "command" != "secret-tool" all bypass the blocklist. TestLeadingCommand (bash_test.go:75) explicitly documents that mid-string `;` is NOT split (`"echo;hi":"echo;hi"`), and TestBashBlocklist (bash_test.go:32-42) only exercises the bare `banned + args` form — no absolute-path/prefix/separator cases, and there is no bash_security_test.go, so the property is not already asserted. Not wrong-layer: the model's bash command is attacker-controlled in the harness threat model. No contract contradiction: the doc (harness-architecture.md:223, 1729-1732) states the blocklist exists to "prevent the obvious credential-exfiltration paths," and a full path `/usr/bin/security` is exactly such an obvious path, so the proposed filepath.Base+segment-split fix strengthens rather than contradicts the documented intent. Severity tempered to P2 (not higher) because both the code comment (bash.go:20-22) and docs frame the blocklist as defense-in-depth behind the authoritative permission middleware (Bash is Mutating -> default "ask", permission.go:11-12) and the credential-helper subprocess holds provider tokens out of the agent's memory (docs:1723-1727); the bypass only re-exposes user OS-keychain exfil, not the harness's own secrets.

findings.property: A FileField URL that a browser resolves as a javascript/vbscript/data scheme must be rejected by Validate.
findings.title: URL scheme guard misses leading C0 control bytes
findings.attack: Submit a FileField via untrusted JSON with URL set to a leading C0 control followed by javascript: (e.g. 0x0C, 0x0B, or 0x01 then 'javascript:alert(1)'). Validate() returns nil. A downstream renderer drops it into href/src; the browser strips the leading C0 control per the WHATWG URL spec and executes the javascript scheme, yielding stored XSS.
findings.evidence: isUnsafeURLScheme's strings.Map only removes tab, LF, CR, and NUL, and the following strings.TrimLeft only trims space. Any leading C0 control in 0x01-0x08, 0x0B, 0x0C, 0x0E-0x1F survives, so strings.HasPrefix(lower, 'javascript:') is false. The sibling filefield_security_test.go (TestFileField_RejectsJavaScriptScheme) only covers tab/LF/CR/NUL. Browsers strip ALL leading C0-control-or-space bytes (0x00-0x20) before scheme resolution, so the guard is weaker than the browser it claims to mirror per its own doc-comment.
findings.proposedFix: Strip every rune below 0x20 everywhere in the strings.Map closure (not just tab/LF/CR/NUL), or after mapping TrimLeft a cutset containing all C0 controls plus space, matching the spec's 'remove leading C0 control or space' rule.

verdict.reason: Confirmed empirically: isUnsafeURLScheme (framework/file/filefield.go:113-129) only removes \t \n \r \0 in strings.Map and only trims leading space via TrimLeft. A leading C0 control in 0x01-0x08/0x0B/0x0C/0x0E-0x1F survives, so HasPrefix(lower,"javascript:") is false — I ran the exact function and "\x0Cjavascript:alert(1)" returns unsafe=false, so Validate returns nil. The function's own doc-comment claims it "mirrors browser URL normalization," but WHATWG strips leading C0-control-or-space before scheme resolution, so the browser would execute the javascript scheme; fixing it fulfills the contract rather than contradicting it. The sibling filefield_security_test.go TestFileField_RejectsJavaScriptScheme only asserts \t \n \r \x00 / space / plain forms — none of the surviving C0 bytes — so the property is not covered. Severity refined to P2: this is field validation; reaching href/src drop-in is downstream and unproven here, though it is the function's stated purpose.

findings.property: Error responses must not leak raw database driver text to clients.
findings.title: Cursor list path leaks raw driver error text
findings.attack: Send GET /<table>?cursor= (empty cursor triggers cursor mode per crud.go:289). If the underlying query/scan fails (e.g. DB connection dropped, a filter value coerced into a type the column rejects, or a Postgres placeholder mismatch), the raw driver error string is returned verbatim in the JSON body.
findings.evidence: serveCursorList writes `"query failed: "+err.Error()` (line 117), `"scan failed: "+err.Error()` (line 124), and `"include failed: "+err.Error()` (line 129) directly to the client. By contrast ServeStreamingList (crud_stream.go:55,78) and the non-cursor List path redact to 'internal server error'. The error-leak tests (error_leak_security_test.go) close the DB then assert no 'database is closed'/'sql:' text leaks for List() and Get(), but never exercise the ?cursor mode, so this surface is uncovered.
findings.proposedFix: Mirror the streaming path: log.Printf the real error and writeJSONError(w, 500, "internal server error") for the query and scan failures; for the include failure use a generic 'include failed' message without err.Error(). Keep the 400 'invalid cursor' message (that one is user-actionable and carries no driver text).

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code: serveCursorList writes "query failed: "+err.Error() (crud_cursor.go:117), "scan failed: "+err.Error() (line 124), and "include failed: "+err.Error() (line 129) directly to the client. Cursor mode is reachable by any client via ?cursor (crud.go:289) and the query builds PostgreSQL $N placeholders, so a driver/connection error surfaces raw text. The peer paths redact: crud_stream.go:58-60/81-83 and the non-cursor List path crud.go:358-359/366-367 log.Printf the error and write only "internal server error", and crud.go:672-679 documents that driver text MUST be hidden — so the cursor path violates the codebase's own stated contract rather than relying on an escape hatch. Coverage: error_leak_security_test.go only asserts no-leak for List() count and Get(), never cursor mode; and deep_security_test.go's skipIfPostgresPlaceholderError (lines 34-36, 537, 1403, 1597) actually pattern-matches the leaked "query failed"/"scan failed" strings as a SKIP signal, confirming the leak exists and is unguarded. Not wrong-layer (runtime driver errors, not developer config). P2 info leak of driver internals is appropriate.

findings.property: Per-request filename sanitization work must be bounded by a length cap before O(n) memory-heavy passes run, not after.
findings.title: SanitizeFilename does unbounded work before length cap
findings.attack: POST a multipart upload whose Content-Disposition filename is a ~9 MiB string of '.' characters (e.g. "x" + strings.Repeat(".",9<<20) + ".jpg"). Multipart filenames are metadata and are NOT counted against ParseMultipartForm's maxMemory; the stdlib cap is 10 MiB per MIME header. For any app with a typical large MaxSize (image/file uploads commonly 10-50 MiB) the giant filename fits under http.MaxBytesReader(MaxSize+slack) with a near-empty content part, and when Config.MaxSize==0 (documented 'no limit') it is reachable up to the full 10 MiB header cap.
findings.evidence: SanitizeFilename runs stripControlBytes (builds a new string), strings.ReplaceAll, strings.NewReplacer.Replace, and crucially neutraliseInteriorExecExts -> strings.Split(name, ".") on the FULL untruncated input (validate.go:101-148). The MaxFilenameBytes (255) cap is the very LAST step (validate.go:163-171). Measured: a 9 MiB all-dots name produces a ~9.4M-element []string from strings.Split in ~88ms plus the join, i.e. tens of MB allocated and >100ms CPU per request — a request-amplified CPU/allocation DoS. The handler calls SanitizeFilename(header.Filename) at upload.go:131 with the raw attacker filename. Sibling tests (upload_bypass_security_test.go TestSanitize_TruncatesOnRuneBoundary, max ~1200-byte inputs) only assert OUTPUT length, never the unbounded-work-before-truncation cost; no test exercises a multi-MiB filename.
findings.proposedFix: Cap the input at the very top of SanitizeFilename before any pass: if len(name) > someBound (e.g. 4*MaxFilenameBytes to leave slack for multibyte/escaped sequences) hard-truncate to that bound first (on a rune boundary), or reject. Alternatively reject filenames longer than a small bound at the handler before calling SanitizeFilename. The order must be: bound length first, then the per-rune / split passes.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code. SanitizeFilename (validate.go:94) runs stripControlBytes, strings.ReplaceAll, replacer.Replace, and neutraliseInteriorExecExts -> strings.Split(name,".") (validate.go:286) on the FULL untruncated input, and only applies the MaxFilenameBytes=255 cap as the very last step (validate.go:163). The handler passes the raw multipart header.Filename to it (upload.go:131). The filename is a MIME-header value not counted against ParseMultipartForm's maxMemory (the package's own comment confirms this, upload.go:14-19), so it is bounded only by the stdlib 10 MiB per-MIME-header cap. When Config.MaxSize==0 ("no limit", upload.go:47) the MaxBytesReader guard is skipped (upload.go:69) and a ~9 MiB filename is fully reachable; for typical large MaxSize image/file configs it also fits under MaxSize+4KiB slack. I measured a "x"+Repeat(".",9<<20)+".jpg" input taking ~80ms CPU and producing a ~9.4M-element split slice (tens of MB transient alloc) per request before being truncated to 255 bytes — a genuine request-amplified CPU/allocation DoS. No sibling *_security_test.go asserts bounded work before truncation: upload_bypass_security_test.go uses <=~1200-byte inputs and only checks output length/UTF-8 validity (lines 166-177), and upload_resource_security_test.go only covers body-size bounding and temp-file cleanup, never filename length. The proposed fix (length-bound before the O(n) passes) does not contradict any documented escape hatch. P2: amplified per-request DoS bounded at 10 MiB by the stdlib, requiring MaxSize==0 or a large MaxSize, no panic/auth impact.

findings.property: State-changing data-fui-rpc requests must carry the CSRF token via one of the framework's documented channels (X-CSRF-Token header or urlencoded _csrf field).
findings.title: Primary RPC dispatcher omits CSRF token
findings.attack: Host app follows the documented CSRF setup (auth.CSRF middleware + <meta name="csrf-token">, auth.md lines 304-321). Any data-fui-rpc button or form (a JSON-bodied POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) is dispatched by dispatchRPC, which sets only X-FUI-Widget and Content-Type headers (lines 127-129) and, for POST forms, serializes fields to a JSON body (lines 122-123). Neither documented token channel is satisfied: there is no X-CSRF-Token header, and a hidden _csrf form field is sent as JSON (not as the application/x-www-form-urlencoded `_csrf` the middleware parses). A button RPC sends no body fields at all.
findings.evidence: runtime.js dispatchRPC builds `headers` with only X-FUI-Widget (128) and Content-Type (129) and never reads `meta[name="csrf-token"]`; grep for csrf/X-CSRF in runtime.js returns nothing. By contrast the sibling action modules src/toggleaction.js (csrfHeaders(), lines 51-58) and src/optimisticaction.js (lines 62-72) both forward X-CSRF-Token — exactly the fix agent-notes.md line 98 records was applied to OptimisticAction after a security review. The widget-scoped dispatchRPC (widgets.js ~307-326) and src/infinitescroll.js also omit it. auth.md:319-321 states the middleware accepts the token only as a urlencoded `_csrf` field or the X-CSRF-Token header; a JSON RPC body provides neither. Result: with the documented CSRF middleware enabled, the framework's flagship RPC primitive is rejected for every state-changing call; without it, those endpoints are CSRF-unprotected.
findings.proposedFix: Add a shared csrfHeaders() helper (mirroring toggleaction.js) that reads `meta[name="csrf-token"]` and sets X-CSRF-Token; call it in both the core dispatchRPC (runtime.js ~127) and the widget-scoped dispatchRPC (widgets.js ~325), and in infinitescroll's fetch. Keep credentials same-origin. Document the meta-tag requirement so the header path works for JSON-bodied RPCs.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code: dispatchRPC (core-ui/runtime/runtime.js:84) builds headers with only X-FUI-Widget (line 128) and Content-Type:application/json (line 129) and never reads meta[name="csrf-token"]; the submit listener (line 266) routes data-fui-rpc forms here as JSON bodies (line 123). The CSRF middleware (core/middleware/csrf.go:232-249) accepts the token only via the X-CSRF-Token header or, for form content-types, the _csrf field via parseFormField — but parseFormField is gated behind isFormContentType (line 233), which is false for application/json, so a JSON RPC satisfies neither channel and is rejected 403. The same gap exists in the widget-scoped dispatchRPC (src/widgets.js:325) and infinitescroll.js (line 53). This directly violates the documented binding rule in core-ui/ARCHITECTURE.md:713 ('State-changing fetches from runtime modules must forward the page's CSRF token ... OptimisticAction is the canonical example'), which toggleaction.js (csrfHeaders, line 51) and optimisticaction.js (line 68) follow but dispatchRPC does not — so fixing it does NOT contradict any escape hatch, it satisfies the contract. Not already covered: no *_security_test.go exists in core-ui/runtime, and the only CSRF e2e (framework/ui_e2e_test.go:544) exercises a plain native HTML form WITHOUT data-fui-rpc (so it bypasses dispatchRPC entirely) — the dispatchRPC JSON path is untested. P2 (not P1) because the hard break only manifests once the host enables the documented CSRF middleware (developer config), and the CSRF-exposure variant only matters when that middleware is absent; either way it breaks the framework's documented secure RPC path.

findings.property: Shutdown must release all resources (goroutines/contexts) it created per session.
findings.title: Per-session persistLoop goroutine and context leak on Shutdown
findings.attack: Create N sessions over the harness lifetime (CreateSession). Each spawns a persistLoop goroutine bound to a context.WithCancel whose cancel func is dropped (`_ = cancel`). Harness.Shutdown only calls h.Sessions.Close() and never cancels these contexts, closes the engine buses, or unregisters engines — so every session's goroutine + context leaks for the process lifetime.
findings.evidence: CreateSession (harness.go:267-270) does `ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background()); _ = cancel // canceled by Shutdown via bus.Close cascade` then `go h.persistLoop(ctx, ch)`. Harness.Shutdown (harness.go:319-330) only closes h.Sessions; it never calls Mux.UnregisterEngine, engine Bus.Close (engine/events.go:114), nor cancel(). The 'canceled by Shutdown via bus.Close cascade' comment is provably false — no such cascade exists.
findings.proposedFix: Track each session's cancel func (and engine) on the Harness; in Shutdown, call every cancel(), UnregisterEngine, and bus.Close so persistLoop goroutines exit and channels close.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code. CreateSession (harness.go:267-270) does `ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background()); _ = cancel` then `go h.persistLoop(ctx, ch)`. persistLoop (harness.go:312) blocks on `for env := range ch`, which only terminates when the bus subscription channel closes — that requires sub.cancel() via Bus.Close() (engine/events.go:114-128) or parent-ctx cancellation. But the parent cancel is discarded and Harness.Shutdown (harness.go:319-330) calls only h.Sessions.Close(); it never calls Bus.Close(), Mux.UnregisterEngine, or Catalog.UnregisterEngine. Grep confirms the only non-test Bus.Close() is engine/loop.go:342 on a locally-created subBus, so the inline comment 'canceled by Shutdown via bus.Close cascade' is false — no such cascade exists. Each session thus leaks a goroutine + context for the process lifetime. No *_security_test.go exists in the package, so the property is unasserted. Not attacker-vs-developer-input confusion and the fix matches (rather than contradicts) the documented intent. Slow resource leak proportional to session count; P2.

findings.property: Dequeue must never lose jobs it has already RPop'd off the main list.
findings.title: RedisQueue.Dequeue silently loses jobs on a single malformed message
findings.attack: Enqueue (or have any producer write to the Redis list) one job whose stored value is not valid Job JSON, behind one or more valid jobs that don't match the requested type filter. On unmarshal failure Dequeue returns immediately and drops every job already accumulated in `skipped`.
findings.evidence: redis.go:91-94: `if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(data), &job); err != nil { return Job{}, fmt.Errorf(...) }` returns without the `for _, s := range skipped { LPush }` re-enqueue that every other exit path (lines 84-88, 150-156 region) performs. Any jobs RPop'd into `skipped` earlier in the loop are permanently lost. The skipped slice is also unbounded — a rare-type filter against a large list RPops the entire queue into memory (OOM).
findings.proposedFix: Re-enqueue the accumulated `skipped` slice before returning on the unmarshal error (move the malformed entry to the dead-letter queue instead of dropping it); and bound the skipped drain or use a server-side type filter so a type miss cannot pull the whole queue into process memory.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code. battery/queue/redis.go Dequeue accumulates RPop'd non-matching jobs into `skipped` (line 99), but the unmarshal-failure exit at lines 92-94 returns without the `for _, s := range skipped { LPush }` re-enqueue that both other exit paths perform (RPop-error path 84-88, success path 111-114). Trigger: with a type filter, iteration 1 pops a valid non-matching job (→skipped), iteration 2 pops a malformed entry → return at line 93, permanently losing the already-popped valid job. This directly violates the code's own stated invariant ("Re-enqueue skipped jobs so we don't lose them", line 84). No *_security_test.go exists in the package, and queue_test.go (TestRedisDequeueTypeFilter etc.) never exercises a malformed entry behind skipped jobs, so the property is uncovered. Refined to P2 (not P1) because the trigger requires a malformed/corrupted list entry — an edge condition rather than routine request input — but the silent permanent loss of unrelated valid jobs is a genuine durability/correctness defect, not speculation. The secondary unbounded-`skipped` OOM concern is also real but weaker.

findings.property: Chunking a document must be linear in the document length, not quadratic, so a single bounded request cannot trigger disproportionate CPU/allocation.
findings.title: FixedWindow.Chunk recomputes byte offset O(N^2)
findings.attack: POST /embed/index with one document whose `text` is ~1M characters (well within the 1 MiB body cap). The default chunker is NewFixedWindow(512,64) (step 448), producing ~2200 windows; chunking that single doc churns on the order of N^2/step bytes (~1e9) of string allocation per request. A few concurrent /index calls drive multi-GB allocation churn.
findings.evidence: Inside the per-window loop (lines 49-63), line 57 computes `byteStart := len(string(runes[:start]))`, re-materializing the entire prefix `runes[:start]` as a fresh string on every iteration. Since `start` grows linearly across ~N/step iterations and each prefix copy costs O(start), total work/allocation is O(N^2/step). The body cap (battery/embed/routes.go maxRequestBody = 1 MiB) bounds N but does not prevent the quadratic amplification per request. No bench/test exercises a large single document (bench_test.go uses tiny inputs).
findings.proposedFix: Track the byte offset incrementally instead of recomputing the prefix each iteration: maintain a running `byteStart` and advance it by the byte length of the runes consumed by `step` each loop (e.g. compute `len(string(runes[prevStart:start]))` once per step and accumulate), or convert offsets via a single precomputed rune->byte index table. This makes chunking O(N).

verdict.reason: Confirmed in battery/embed/chunker.go:57 — `byteStart := len(string(runes[:start]))` re-materializes the entire growing prefix every loop iteration, giving O(N^2/step) allocation. The path is triggerable: routes.go indexHandler -> idx.Add -> index.go:88 chunker.Chunk, with the default chunker NewFixedWindow(512,64) (embed.go:181, step 448) and fully attacker-controlled doc.Text bounded only by the 1 MiB body cap (routes.go:14). I ran a benchmark: doubling N from 100K->200K->400K runes scaled time ~28ms->104ms->425ms and allocations ~12.5MB->47.5MB->184MB (roughly 4x per doubling), confirming quadratic, not linear, behavior; extrapolating to the ~1M-rune cap yields ~1GB+ allocation and seconds of CPU per request. The sibling routes_security_test.go only asserts auth, error-leak suppression, content-type, and oversized-body (413) — it does not assert linear-time chunking. No doc-comment promises a particular offset-computation strategy, so the incremental-offset fix contradicts no documented contract. P2 is appropriate: cost per request is bounded by the 1 MiB cap, so impact requires repeated/concurrent requests rather than a single fatal request.

findings.property: The MaxFilenameBytes length cap must truncate the filename on a UTF-8 rune boundary so the resulting storage key / metadata is always valid UTF-8.
findings.title: Filename length-cap splits multibyte runes -> invalid UTF-8 key
findings.attack: Upload a file whose name is a long non-ASCII string, e.g. filename strings.Repeat("世", 200) + ".jpg" (600+ bytes). SanitizeFilename truncates to 255 bytes at a byte boundary, slicing through a 3-byte rune.
findings.evidence: Lines 159-167: `name = name[:MaxFilenameBytes-len(ext)] + ext` slices `name` at a raw byte index with no utf8.DecodeLastRune / boundary check. Verified: SanitizeFilename(repeat("世",200)+".jpg") returns len=255 with utf8.ValidString==false (orphaned 0xc3/0xe4 lead byte). Driven through Handler the corrupted string becomes the storage key (os.OpenFile) and the JSON Metadata.Key. On macOS/HFS+ the OpenFile then fails and the whole upload returns 500 (confirmed status=500); on ext4 it silently stores a key with invalid UTF-8 that later breaks utf8mb4 DB columns / JSON consumers. The cap is documented but rune-safe truncation is not — the implementation contradicts the 'still round-trips through MIME detection' intent in the doc-comment.
findings.proposedFix: Truncate on a rune boundary: compute the byte budget (MaxFilenameBytes-len(ext)), then walk back from that index with utf8.DecodeLastRuneInString until the prefix is valid UTF-8 (or use a rune-aware truncate helper), before re-appending ext.

verdict.reason: Verified in core/upload/validate.go:165 — `name = name[:MaxFilenameBytes-len(ext)] + ext` slices at a raw byte index. Empirically, SanitizeFilename(strings.Repeat("世",200)+".jpg") returns a 255-byte string with utf8.ValidString==false (orphaned 0xe4 lead byte). The result flows through upload.go:92-110 to become both the on-disk key (LocalStorage.Save -> os.OpenFile keeps raw bytes) and Metadata.Key, which json.NewEncoder mangles to U+FFFD (verified: on-disk 616263e4 vs JSON 616263efbfbd) — so the returned key no longer maps to the stored file, and the invalid-UTF-8 string breaks utf8mb4 DB columns. No sibling *_security_test.go asserts UTF-8 validity of the truncated name (grep found only null-byte truncation, a different property). header.Filename is genuinely attacker-controlled multipart input, so this is the right layer; the doc-comment's "round-trips through MIME detection" intent is undermined, not contradicted by, the fix. Severity P2: requires a long non-ASCII filename (edge input) and yields key/metadata corruption rather than RCE or authz bypass.

findings.property: Dependency tracking must be goroutine-scoped so concurrent reactive evaluations do not share state.
findings.title: Global currentCtx causes data race in concurrent signal tracking
findings.attack: Two HTTP requests (or any two goroutines) each construct a Computed/Effect at the same time: NewComputed -> startTracking sets the process-global currentCtx, then runs compute() with no lock held; the other goroutine's Signal.Get() -> trackDependency writes into the SAME global currentCtx.dependencies map.
findings.evidence: tracker.go uses package-level `var currentCtx *trackingContext`. startTracking (line 21-26) sets `currentCtx = ctx` then unlocks before compute() runs; trackDependency (line 38-44) writes `currentCtx.dependencies[sig]`. I added a concurrent NewComputed+Get test and `go test -race` reported a confirmed DATA RACE on the dependencies map (concurrent map iter/write across goroutines via computed.go:33 and tracker.go:42). Result: a Computed subscribes to another goroutine's signals (cross-request dependency leakage) or panics on concurrent map access.
findings.proposedFix: Make tracking goroutine-local instead of a process-global. Either store the active trackingContext stack in a goroutine-keyed structure, pass the context explicitly through Get/Compute, or hold the tracker mutex for the entire startTracking->compute->stop window (serializing tracked evaluations). The current scheme (set-global, release-lock, run compute) is fundamentally unsafe under concurrency.

verdict.reason: Verified: tracker.go uses a process-global `var currentCtx *trackingContext` (line 14). startTracking() releases trackingMu at line 26 before NewComputed runs compute() (computed.go:29), while trackDependency writes `currentCtx.dependencies[sig]` (tracker.go:42) under the mutex but into whatever global context another goroutine may have just overwritten. I reproduced a confirmed `go test -race` DATA RACE at exactly tracker.go:42 vs computed.go:33 with two goroutines each doing NewComputed+Get. This violates the package doc contract "All primitives are safe for concurrent use" (doc.go:12), so the fix upholds the contract rather than contradicting it. The sibling signal_test.go TestConcurrentAccess only exercises concurrent Set/Get on a single Signal — it never touches the tracking-context path, so this is not already covered; there is no *_security_test.go in the package. Not wrong-layer: two goroutines building computeds is ordinary public-API use. Refined to P2 (not P1) because the package is not yet wired into any request-handling path (only references are a docs string in screens_pages.go:322 and a linter allow-list entry), so the "two HTTP requests" attacker framing overstates current reachability; impact is a panic / cross-context dependency leakage on concurrent use, not an auth/data-exposure bypass.

findings.property: A panic inside a registered MCP tool handler must not crash the server or abort the JSON-RPC response.
findings.title: MCP tool-handler panic is never recovered
findings.attack: Send a JSON-RPC tools/call whose `arguments` trigger a panic in a tool handler — e.g. a handler that does params["id"].(string) and the attacker supplies {"id":123}, or writes to the nil `arguments` map. The panic is attacker-reachable because params.Arguments comes straight from the request body (tools.go handleToolsCall).
findings.evidence: Neither callTool (server.go:111 `result, err := t.Handler(ctx, params)`) nor HandleRequest (protocol.go) nor any transport entry (transport.go ServeHTTP/ssePostHandler/ServeStdio) wraps the handler call in recover(). `grep recover|panic core/mcp/` returns nothing. Over ServeStdio the panic unwinds the scan loop and crashes the whole process (the canonical MCP stdio deployment). Over HTTP net/http's per-request recover fires but the client gets a reset connection / truncated SSE frame instead of a JSON-RPC error, and ssePostHandler already wrote 200 + text/event-stream. Contrast core/handler/handler.go HandlerAdapter which deliberately defers a recover().
findings.proposedFix: Wrap the handler invocation in callTool with defer/recover, converting a recovered panic into *RPCError{Code: ErrInternalError, Message: "internal tool error"} (do NOT echo the panic value, to avoid info leak). Keeps ServeStdio alive and returns a well-formed JSON-RPC error on every transport.

verdict.reason: Verified: no recover() exists anywhere in core/mcp/ (grep returns nothing). callTool (server.go:111 `result, err := t.Handler(ctx, params)`), HandleRequest (protocol.go:46), and all transports (transport.go ServeHTTP/ssePostHandler/ServeStdio) invoke the handler unguarded. Attacker-controlled JSON-RPC `arguments` flow straight into the handler via tools.go:53 `s.callTool(ctx, params.Name, params.Arguments)`. The core/mcp.Server is the real stdio deployment: kiln/agent/mcp/mcp.go NewServer returns *core/mcp.Server and cmd/kiln/main.go:262 calls its ServeStdio over os.Stdin/os.Stdout — a panic in a handler unwinds the bufio scan loop (transport.go:119) and crashes the whole process, with no net/http recover net on stdio. The sibling core/handler/handler.go:20 deliberately defers recover() at its handler boundary, so the unguarded MCP boundary is an inconsistency, not an intentional escape hatch (no doc-comment sanctions it). The transport_security_test.go asserts only body-size, content-type, no-store, and SSE injection — not handler-panic recovery. Severity reduced to P2 because the trigger is gated on a developer-registered handler actually panicking (e.g. unchecked type assertion on params); it is real and attacker-reachable but is hardening of a boundary around developer code rather than a direct injection/authz bypass.

findings.property: A response-writer wrapper must preserve the optional interfaces (Hijacker, Pusher) the underlying writer implements so streaming/upgrade handlers keep working downstream.
findings.title: metrics/tracing writers drop Hijacker and Pusher
findings.attack: Place MetricsMiddleware (or Tracing()) ahead of a handler that performs a WebSocket upgrade or calls w.(http.Hijacker) — e.g. MetricsMiddleware is wired globally in framework/e2e_full_test.go:159. The hijack type-assertion now fails because the request is wrapped in metricsResponseWriter, which only re-exposes Flush().
findings.evidence: metricsResponseWriter (metrics.go:163-194) embeds http.ResponseWriter and overrides WriteHeader/Write/Flush but NOT Hijack/Push, and implements no Unwrap() for http.ResponseController. Same for tracingResponseWriter (tracing.go:96-122). The sibling logging.go solves exactly this with reveal()/wrapResponseWriter conditionally re-exposing Flusher+Hijacker+Pusher, and logging_hardening_test.go asserts Hijack passthrough — but no metrics_test.go/tracing_test.go asserts it, so the regression is unguarded. A hijack/upgrade handler behind metrics/tracing silently loses its upgrade path.
findings.proposedFix: Reuse the logging.go reveal() pattern (or add Hijack()/Push() passthrough plus Unwrap() http.ResponseWriter) on metricsResponseWriter and tracingResponseWriter so they expose exactly the interfaces the wrapped writer supports.

verdict.reason: Verified: metricsResponseWriter (core/middleware/metrics.go:163-194) and tracingResponseWriter (tracing.go:96-122) embed http.ResponseWriter and override only Write/WriteHeader/Flush — neither re-exposes Hijack/Push nor provides Unwrap(). The wrap is applied globally via MetricsMiddleware (metrics.go:54-67, wired with r.Use in framework/e2e_full_test.go) and Tracing(). The framework actually depends on Hijacker passthrough: core/stream/websocket.go:136 and framework/harness/control/ws/ws.go:109 both do w.(http.Hijacker) and error if it's missing, so a WS-upgrade handler behind metrics/tracing loses its upgrade path. This is an inconsistency with the package's own contract — timeout.go:113 preserves Hijacker and logging.go uses the full reveal()/hijackWriter/pushWriter pattern; the Flush() doc-comment (metrics.go:185-189) explicitly states the wrapper must preserve optional interfaces, so dropping Hijack contradicts its own stated intent rather than being a documented escape hatch. No metrics_test.go/tracing_test.go or *_security_test.go asserts Hijacker (only logging_hardening_test.go:35 does, for the logging wrapper). P2 is fair: real and triggerable, but realization requires a host to mount a hijack/WS handler behind the global metrics/tracing middleware.

findings.property: A transaction must be either committed or rolled back on every exit path, including when fn panics.
findings.title: App.InTx leaks tx connection + row locks when fn panics
findings.attack: Caller passes an fn that panics (e.g. a seeder/batch/multi-entity write path that nil-derefs or type-asserts on edge data). The framework's recovery middleware (core/middleware/recovery.go) catches the panic at the HTTP boundary so the process survives, but App.InTx unwound without running tx.Rollback() or tx.Commit().
findings.evidence: tx.go lines 31-40: tx, _ := a.DB.BeginTx(...); if err := fn(txCtx, tx); err != nil { _ = tx.Rollback(); return err }; return tx.Commit(). There is no `defer` guarding the panic path — a panic in fn unwinds past both Rollback and Commit, so the *sql.Tx is never closed. The connection stays checked out of the pool (and any rows it locked stay locked) until the sql package's finalizer or pool lifetime reclaims it. Repeated panics exhaust MaxOpenConns -> DoS. The sibling typed_hooks_security_test.go:123 comment explicitly states the framework relies on 'tx rollback' for the error chain, and recovers HOOK panics into errors precisely so the tx is cleaned up — but App.InTx itself (a public API documented for 'seeders, batch jobs, multi-entity write paths') runs arbitrary fn with no such recovery, so a panic from non-hook code in fn still leaks.
findings.proposedFix: Wrap the tx lifecycle in a defer: `committed := false; defer func(){ if !committed { _ = tx.Rollback() } }()`, set committed=true only after a successful Commit(). Optionally recover-rollback-repanic so the connection is released before the panic continues unwinding to the recovery middleware.

verdict.reason: Verified in framework/tx.go:36-40: InTx calls fn(txCtx, tx) with no defer guard, then conditionally Rollback (line 37) or Commit (line 40). A panic in fn unwinds past both, so the *sql.Tx is never closed — the pooled connection stays checked out and any rows it locked stay locked, leaking until pool/finalizer reclaim; repeated panics exhaust MaxOpenConns (DoS). The three InTx tests (tx_test.go:197/219/243) only exercise success-commit, error-rollback, and tx-in-context; none passes a panicking fn. TestHooks_RecoverPanicsAsErrors (typed_hooks_security_test.go:141) recovers panics in the *hook registry*, a separate path — InTx itself has no recovery, and its doc-comment (lines 23-25) explicitly markets it for seeders/batch/multi-entity writes where edge-data nil-derefs are realistic. The fix (committed-flag defer-rollback) contradicts no documented escape hatch.

findings.property: Two distinct uploads must never resolve to the same storage path, so one upload cannot silently overwrite another.
findings.title: GenerateFilePath uniqueness is timestamp-only, collides
findings.attack: Two clients upload files with the same sanitized filename (both 'avatar.png') to the same entity/field. If time.Now().UnixNano() yields the same value (same nanosecond, or on platforms whose clock does not advance every nanosecond) both produce path uploads/<entity>/<field>/avatar_<ns>.png. The second store.Save overwrites the first with no error; the first user's persisted URL now serves the second user's content.
findings.evidence: uniqueName is fmt.Sprintf('%s_%d%s', nameWithoutExt, time.Now().UnixNano(), ext) -- the only entropy is the wall-clock nanosecond plus the sanitized base name; there is no random/UUID component and no existence check. ProcessFileField calls store.Save unconditionally and the storage backends overwrite an existing key. No sibling test asserts uniqueness/non-collision for GenerateFilePath.
findings.proposedFix: Append a crypto/rand-derived component (hex bytes or UUIDv4) to uniqueName so uniqueness does not depend on clock resolution; optionally also call store.Exists and regenerate on collision.

verdict.reason: Verified in framework/file/filefield.go:371 — uniqueName = fmt.Sprintf("%s_%d%s", nameWithoutExt, time.Now().UnixNano(), ext); the only entropy is the wall-clock nanosecond plus the attacker-supplied sanitized filename, with no random/UUID component and no Exists check. ProcessFileField (line 216-219) calls store.Save unconditionally, and core/upload/local.go:80 opens with os.O_CREATE|os.O_TRUNC (no O_EXCL), so an existing key is silently truncated. The entity/field path segments are constant per field (e.g. uploads/users/avatar/), so two users uploading the same filename collide only on the timestamp. The premise that the clock does not advance every nanosecond is empirically true on this platform: a 1M-call loop showed time.Now().UnixNano() returned the same value as the previous call 96.7% of the time (microsecond resolution on darwin). Under concurrent uploads two requests within one microsecond produce an identical path and the second overwrites the first, serving one user's content from another user's persisted URL. No sibling *_security_test.go asserts uniqueness/non-collision (grep found nothing). The doc-comment at line 359 explicitly promises a "safe, unique file path," so the proposed crypto/rand fix upholds the documented contract rather than contradicting an escape hatch. Severity stays P2 because the cross-user overwrite is probabilistic (requires a same-microsecond collision plus identical sanitized filename), not deterministically attacker-triggerable on demand.

findings.property: Offset pagination must return offset=(page-1)*limit for any valid limit, including limit=1.
findings.title: limit=1 always resets offset to 0, breaking offset pagination
findings.attack: Any client requests ?page=N&limit=1 (e.g. /?page=3&limit=1). All pages return page-1 data; pages 2+ are unreachable.
findings.evidence: The overflow guard reads `if offset < 0 || page > math.MaxInt/limit+1`. When limit==1, `math.MaxInt/1` is math.MaxInt and `+1` wraps at runtime to math.MinInt. The comparison `page > math.MinInt` is true for every parseable page (page>=1), so offset is unconditionally reset to 0. Verified by running ParsePagination: page=3&limit=1 returns offset=0 (expected 2), while limit=2 returns offset=4 and limit=100 returns offset=200 correctly. The existing pagination_security_test.go only exercises limit=MaxPageSize, never limit=1, so this path is untested.
findings.proposedFix: Compute the threshold without the wrapping `+1`: guard with `if limit > 0 && page > 1 && page-1 > math.MaxInt/limit { offset = 0 }`, or check overflow after the multiply only via the sign test plus a divide-back check `offset/limit != page-1`. Do not add 1 to a value that can equal math.MaxInt.

verdict.reason: Verified at framework/pagination/pagination.go:113. The guard `if offset < 0 || page > math.MaxInt/limit+1` parses as `page > (math.MaxInt/limit)+1`. When limit==1, `math.MaxInt/1` is math.MaxInt and `+1` overflows to math.MinInt at runtime, so `page > math.MinInt` is true for every parseable page (page>=1), unconditionally resetting offset to 0. I confirmed this empirically: page=3&limit=1 -> offset=0 (expected 2); limit=2 and limit=100 compute correct offsets. The sibling pagination_security_test.go only asserts `offset < 0` for page=maxInt&limit=MaxPageSize (line 17) and never exercises limit=1 nor asserts a positive offset value, so the property is uncovered. No doc-comment contract is contradicted; the comment at lines 88-89 documents only cursor precedence, and 108-111 documents the overflow intent the buggy line fails to implement. Not attacker-vs-developer mislayering: page/limit are attacker-controlled query params. Genuine but low-impact (availability/correctness of offset pagination, pages 2+ unreachable at limit=1), so P2 stands.

findings.property: A type-filtered Dequeue must never silently drop the valid, non-matching jobs it drained while searching.
findings.title: MemoryQueue type-filter Dequeue silently drops drained jobs
findings.attack: Worker pool draining: a near-full 1024-slot jobChan with active producers/workers. A consumer calls Dequeue(ctx, "rare-type") which RPops non-matching jobs into `skipped`, then re-enqueues them via the NON-BLOCKING enqueueInternal. While it was draining, concurrent producers refilled the buffer, so the re-enqueue select hits `default` and the drained jobs are discarded.
findings.evidence: In Dequeue the type-filter branch appends every non-match to `skipped` (memory.go:157) and re-enqueues with `_ = q.enqueueInternal(s)` (lines 153,161,166). enqueueInternal (memory.go:189-196) is non-blocking: `select { case q.jobChan <- job: return nil; default: return ErrQueueClosed }`. The error is discarded with `_ =`, so when the buffered channel has no free slot at the instant of re-enqueue, the drained-but-non-matching job is permanently lost. This is the exact job-loss class that the Redis Dequeue variant was patched for (requeueSkipped + DLQ); the in-memory variant still drops on a full channel.
findings.proposedFix: Re-enqueue drained non-matching jobs through a blocking send (respecting ctx) rather than the non-blocking enqueueInternal, or push them onto an internal holdover slice/deque that is re-fed before returning. At minimum, do not discard the enqueueInternal error — surface it so the caller knows jobs were lost.

verdict.reason: Verified in battery/queue/memory.go: the type-filtered Dequeue drain loop (lines 147-170) RPops non-matching jobs into `skipped`, then re-enqueues them via the non-blocking `enqueueInternal` (lines 153,161,166) whose `select{ case q.jobChan<-job: ...; default: return ErrQueueClosed }` (lines 190-195) silently drops the job when the 1024-slot jobChan is full, and the error is discarded with `_ =`. The drain frees slots, but concurrent Enqueue producers can refill the bounded channel during the drain window, so a re-enqueue hits `default` and the drained-but-valid job is permanently lost — no DLQ, no holdover, no surfaced error. This is the same job-loss class the Redis variant was explicitly patched for (redis.go requeueSkipped + DLQ, asserted by TestRedisDequeueKeepsSkippedOnBadJSON), but no security test asserts it for MemoryQueue (TestMemory* covers only panic survival; TestDequeueByType is single-job happy path). The doc-comment at line 139 promises re-enqueue, so a fix upholds rather than contradicts the contract. Refined to P2: real correctness/job-loss defect requiring concurrent producers + type-filtered Dequeue, not direct attacker input.

findings.property: A partial (206) response must never be stored under, and replayed for, a key that full-content requests also resolve to.
findings.title: 206 Range response poisons cache for full GETs
findings.attack: Attacker (or any client) sends `GET /file` with `Range: bytes=0-5`. The origin (e.g. http.ServeContent) returns `206 Partial Content` with a truncated body. isStoreable treats 206 as storeable (200<=206<400, no Set-Cookie, no private), and the cache key `GET:/file?` contains neither the Range header nor a Vary dimension for it. A subsequent victim `GET /file` (no Range) gets X-Cache=HIT and receives the truncated 206 body+status instead of the full document.
findings.evidence: baseKey at middleware.go:58 is `r.Method+":"+r.URL.Path+"?"+r.URL.RawQuery` — Range is not part of the key. isStoreable (middleware.go:122-144) only rejects status <200 or >=400, so 206 passes. There is no `Range`/`Content-Range`/`Accept-Ranges`/206 guard anywhere in the package (grep confirms none). captureVariant only keys on response Vary, which a Range handler does not set for Range. So the partial variant overwrites/serves the full-request key.
findings.proposedFix: In CacheMiddleware, refuse to serve-from or write-to cache when the request carries a `Range` header, and in isStoreable reject responses with StatusCode==206 (and/or any response carrying a `Content-Range` header). Optionally fold the presence/value of Range into the cache key.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code. baseKey (middleware.go:58) is `r.Method+":"+r.URL.Path+"?"+r.URL.RawQuery` — the Range request header is not part of the key, and captureVariant (line 207) only keys on the response Vary header, which a Range-serving origin (e.g. http.ServeContent) does not set as `Vary: Range`. isStoreable (lines 122-144) rejects only status <200 or >=400, so a 206 passes (200<=206<400); there is no Content-Range/Accept-Ranges/206 guard anywhere in the package (grep confirms zero matches). Thus a client-controlled `Range: bytes=0-5` produces a 206 truncated body stored under the bare `GET:/file?` key with Vary nil, and variantMatches with nil vary returns true for any later plain GET, which gets X-Cache=HIT plus the truncated 206 body and status. Not developer config — Range is attacker/client input. cache_security_test.go asserts Set-Cookie/private/no-store/no-cache/Vary-*/5xx and Vary honoring, but no 206/Range property, so not already covered. The doc comment "only 2xx/3xx responses are stored" describes the buggy behavior (206 is 2xx) rather than an intentional escape hatch, so the proposed fix (reject 206/Content-Range, bypass cache on Range requests) refines the contract rather than contradicting it.

findings.property: A response-writer wrapper must preserve the optional interfaces (http.Hijacker) of the writer it wraps so downstream handlers can still upgrade the connection.
findings.title: log access wrapper drops http.Hijacker, breaks WebSocket upgrades
findings.attack: Mount battery/log (which installs accessMiddleware on every route via app.Use in Plugin.Init) and register any WebSocket / connection-hijacking route. A client opens the WS handshake; the handler calls w.(http.Hijacker) which now fails against countingResponseWriter, so core/stream/websocket.go returns "response writer does not support hijacking" and the upgrade fails for every request.
findings.evidence: countingResponseWriter (battery/log/middleware.go:112-147) embeds http.ResponseWriter and implements only WriteHeader, Write, and Flush. It deliberately added Flush (with a comment mirroring core/middleware.metricsResponseWriter) but never added Hijack. accessMiddleware wraps every response in this type at line 64, and Plugin.Init installs accessMiddleware globally (log.go:205). core/stream/websocket.go:136-138 does `hijacker, ok := w.(http.Hijacker); if !ok { return errors.New("...does not support hijacking") }` — that assertion fails because the wrapper does not forward Hijack. grep confirms battery/log has zero Hijacker support.
findings.proposedFix: Add a Hijack method to countingResponseWriter that forwards to the underlying writer when it implements http.Hijacker, e.g. `func (rw *countingResponseWriter) Hijack() (net.Conn, *bufio.ReadWriter, error) { if h, ok := rw.ResponseWriter.(http.Hijacker); ok { return h.Hijack() }; return nil, nil, errors.New("hijack unsupported") }`. Optionally also forward http.Pusher for completeness, mirroring the same fix already applied to core/middleware metrics writer.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code. countingResponseWriter (battery/log/middleware.go:112-147) embeds http.ResponseWriter and forwards only WriteHeader/Write/Flush — no Hijack — so Go method promotion does not expose Hijack. accessMiddleware wraps it on every route via app.Use (log.go:205), and core/stream/websocket.go:136-139 does `hijacker, ok := w.(http.Hijacker); if !ok { return errors.New("...does not support hijacking") }`, which fails, breaking every WS upgrade under battery/log. The sibling middleware_sse_test.go only asserts http.Flusher preservation, not Hijacker, so the property is NOT already covered. Every other framework writer wrapper (metrics.go:228, tracing.go:128, core/middleware/logging.go) forwards Hijack and has a *_security_test.go asserting it; this wrapper's own Flush comment claims it "Mirrors the fix already present on core/middleware.metricsResponseWriter," yet that writer also forwards Hijack — so this is an incomplete mirror, not a documented escape hatch. No contract contradiction.

findings.property: A Decimal value strictly outside [Min,Max] must be rejected; bounds enforcement must not lose precision for the fixed-precision decimal type.
findings.title: Decimal Min/Max compared via float64 loses precision, bypassing bounds
findings.attack: For a Decimal field with Max set to a large integral cap (e.g. a money/credit cap of 9007199254740992 = 2^53, or any cap above 2^53), submit the JSON string value "9007199254740993" (2^53+1). strconv.ParseFloat rounds it to exactly 2^53, so the check n > *f.Max is false and the over-cap value is accepted and stored. Likewise a value like "1000.0000000000001" against a Max of 1000 — or sub-Min values — can slip past in the precision gap.
findings.evidence: validateDecimal does n, _ := strconv.ParseFloat(s, 64) then compares n < *f.Min and n > *f.Max (validate.go:174,181-186). Decimal is documented in schema.go:11 as "fixed-precision decimal (stored as string)" precisely to avoid float rounding, yet the bound check round-trips through float64. Verified empirically: "9007199254740993" parses to 9007199254740992.0 so n > 2^53 is false (the over-cap value passes). This is the exact precision-loss class the Int validator was hardened against in validate.go:96-104 / floatBoundToInt64, but the fix was never applied to Decimal. The sibling validate_security_test.go covers Decimal non-finite and canonical-form, and Int bound precision (TestIntBoundPrecision), but NOT Decimal bound precision — this property is unasserted.
findings.proposedFix: Compare Decimal values against bounds in exact decimal space rather than float64. Either parse the value and the bound with math/big.Float / big.Rat (or a decimal library) and compare exactly, or keep the bounds as canonical decimal strings and do a sign-aware lexical/big-int comparison. At minimum, mirror the Int approach: when the value is an integral decimal beyond 2^53, fall back to big.Int comparison so values strictly over Max (or under Min) are rejected.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code: validateDecimal (validate.go:174-186) does `n,_:=strconv.ParseFloat(s,64)` then compares `n < *f.Min` / `n > *f.Max` in float64 space. Empirically confirmed: submitting the integer string "9007199254740993" (2^53+1) against Max=2^53 yields n==2^53 so `n > max` is false and the over-cap value is accepted (decimalRe accepts plain integer strings, so it passes the canonical-form gate). This is the exact precision class the Int validator was hardened against via floatBoundToInt64 (validate.go:99-104, comment at 96-98), and which has a dedicated test TestIntBoundPrecision (validate_security_test.go:50) — but the Decimal sibling has NO bound-precision test (only non-finite and canonical-form), so the property is unasserted. Not wrong-layer: the over-cap submitted value is attacker input; the cap is config but defines the boundary, identical to the accepted Int threat model. Not a contract contradiction — schema.go:11 documents Decimal as fixed-precision string "to avoid float rounding," which the float64 bound check undermines. Refined to P2 (not P1): exploitation requires a developer-set cap at or above 2^53, a narrow precondition; the candidate's secondary sub-precision example ("1000.0000000000001" vs Max 1000) did NOT reproduce — it compares as > 1000.0 and is correctly rejected — but the primary integral 2^53 case is real.

findings.property: Keyset (cursor) pagination must order rows in the direction of the comparison so the returned page is the page adjacent to the cursor.
findings.title: Backward cursor emits ASC ORDER BY, returns wrong page
findings.attack: A client requests the previous page via Cursor("id", 100, "backward").Limit(10). The builder emits `WHERE (id < $1) ORDER BY id LIMIT 10` (no direction = ASC), which returns the LOWEST 10 ids in the table (e.g. ids 1..10) instead of the 10 ids immediately before the cursor (90..99). Records between are silently skipped on backward navigation.
findings.evidence: Cursor sets op="<" for dir=="backward" (line 119-121) but unconditionally appends orderClause{column: field, dir: ""} (line 131). Build() emits the ORDER BY with no direction keyword when dir is empty (query.go line 209-213, sanitizeDirection("")==""), so backward pagination always sorts ASC. The existing TestCursorBackward only asserts `id < $1` and never checks ORDER BY direction, so the wrong-order page is uncovered.
findings.proposedFix: In Cursor, set the orderClause direction to "ASC" for forward and "DESC" for backward so the comparison and ordering agree (the caller can reverse the slice for display). Add a test asserting `ORDER BY id DESC` for the backward case.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code: query.go:119-121 sets op="<" for backward but line 131 unconditionally appends orderClause{column: field, dir: ""}, and Build (lines 202-214, sanitizeDirection("")=="") emits ORDER BY with no direction keyword = ASC. I reproduced it: standalone backward emits `WHERE (id < $1) ORDER BY id LIMIT $2` (returns lowest ids, not the page before the cursor). The bug even propagates to the production CRUD path (framework/crud/crud_cursor.go:104-110) which emits `ORDER BY id, id DESC` where the leading bare ASC `id` from Cursor dominates the explicit DESC, so backward pages are wrong there too. TestCursorBackward (query_test.go:139) asserts only `id < $1`, never the ORDER BY direction, so the property is uncovered. This is a correctness bug, not a security defect; the doc-comment (query.go:110-111) documents only the WHERE direction so a fix does not contradict any contract, and no developer-input/wrong-layer concern applies.

findings.property: Parsing of the attacker-controlled Accept-Language header must be bounded so a single request cannot force large allocation + O(n log n) sort work.
findings.title: Unbounded Accept-Language parsing enables request-amplified DoS
findings.attack: Send a request with an Accept-Language header packed with commas, e.g. `a,a,a,...` repeated up to the server's MaxHeaderBytes (~1MB default) — hundreds of thousands of comma-separated segments in one header.
findings.evidence: parseAcceptLanguage (i18n.go:439) does `strings.Split(header, ",")` and builds an `out` slice plus a `tags` slice with no cap on segment count, then sort.SliceStable over all of them (i18n.go:462). Negotiate (i18n.go:413) calls this for every request that reaches the Middleware. A 1MB header of `a,` pairs yields ~500k entries to allocate, normalize (ToLower/TrimSpace per entry), and stable-sort — per request, with no per-request bound beyond the global header limit.
findings.proposedFix: Cap the number of parsed Accept-Language entries (RFC-realistic clients send <50). After splitting, break out of the loop once a small fixed limit (e.g. 32) of valid entries is collected, and/or short-circuit when the header length exceeds a sane bound (a few KB).

verdict.reason: parseAcceptLanguage (core/i18n/i18n.go:439) does strings.Split(header, ",") with no segment cap, appends a normalized (ToLower-allocating, line 460) entry per non-empty q>0 segment — bare tags like "a" default to q=1.0 so they survive the q<=0 filter at line 457 — then sort.SliceStable over the whole slice (line 462). Negotiate (line 413) and Middleware (line 389) call this per request on the attacker-controlled Accept-Language header. A ~1MB header (bounded only by net/http MaxHeaderBytes) of "a," pairs yields ~500k allocations + an O(n log n) sort per request, with no per-request bound. No *_security_test.go exists in core/i18n/ asserting any bound (only i18n_test.go covers q-ordering), and no doc-comment promises unbounded parsing, so a cap contradicts nothing. Severity stays P3: surface is opt-in via WithI18n (app.go:313, not in DefaultMiddleware), work is only O(n log n) and input-bounded by MaxHeaderBytes, so it is a request-amplification vector, not a knockout DoS. This is HTTP header input, not developer config, so not wrong-layer. Could not refute.

findings.property: Per-request metric label dimensions must be bounded so an unauthenticated client cannot grow the in-memory metrics store without limit.
findings.title: Unbounded metrics cardinality via arbitrary HTTP method
findings.attack: Send a stream of requests, each with a distinct but well-formed HTTP method token (Go's net/http accepts any RFC 7230 token as a method, e.g. `A`, `AB`, `AAA`, `X1`, `X2`, ...). The route label is correctly collapsed to "unmatched" for unknown paths, but the method label is taken raw from r.Method, so every distinct method produces a new metricKey{Method,Route,Status} in m.counters that is never evicted.
findings.evidence: MetricsMiddleware (lines 60-64) computes `route` defensively (`if route == "" { route = "unmatched" }`) but passes `r.Method` straight into m.record. record() (lines 69-85) inserts a new *atomic.Uint64 into m.counters for every unseen key under m.mu, with no cap and no reaper. Unlike the idempotency store, which gained WithMemoryStoreMaxEntries + reaping to bound exactly this attack, the metrics map has no bound. net/http does not restrict the method to a known set, so the method dimension is attacker-controlled and unbounded.
findings.proposedFix: Bound the method dimension before recording: either map any method not in a small allowlist (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE/HEAD/OPTIONS/...) to a sentinel like "other", or cap the total number of metricKeys (evict/aggregate beyond a configurable max, mirroring WithMemoryStoreMaxEntries). Apply the same bound to the durations map keyed by route.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code: MetricsMiddleware (metrics.go:60-64) defensively collapses the route label to "unmatched" but passes raw r.Method into m.record; record() (lines 69-85) inserts a new *atomic.Uint64 into m.counters for every unseen metricKey{Method,Route,Status} under m.mu with no cap and no reaper, unlike idempotency.go's WithMemoryStoreMaxEntries/reapLocked. I confirmed via raw TCP socket that Go's net/http delivers arbitrary RFC-7230 method tokens (FOOBAR, X1, ZZZZZ) raw to handlers, and an in-package probe showed 5000 distinct methods produce 5000 never-evicted counter keys. So an unauthenticated client can grow the in-memory metrics store without bound. No sibling *_security_test.go asserts this property and no doc.go escape hatch covers it. Severity refined to P3 (not P2): the leak is modest in-memory map growth (slow DoS, not RCE/data exposure), and MetricsMiddleware/NewMetrics from core/middleware are not wired into any shipped host app — only battery/log's separate MetricsHandler is mounted — so live exploitation requires a developer to opt into mounting this primitive.

findings.property: A _like filter must treat the user value as a literal substring, not as a LIKE pattern, so attacker LIKE metacharacters cannot broaden the match or force expensive scans.
findings.title: LIKE wildcard injection in _like filter (no ESCAPE)
findings.attack: ?field_like=%25 (a single percent) makes `field LIKE '%%%'` match every non-null row, leaking rows the substring filter was meant to exclude. ?field_like=____________ (many underscores) or repeated %_ patterns forces costly pattern scans. Backslashes also alter matching on engines treating it as the escape char.
findings.evidence: In both ApplyToQuery (filter.go:215-216) and ApplyToCountQuery (filter.go:193-194) the OpLike case builds `f.Field+" LIKE $1"` with parameter value `"%"+f.Value+"%"`. The user value f.Value is concatenated raw with no escaping of LIKE metacharacters (% _ \) and the query carries no ESCAPE clause. Parameterization (the $1 placeholder) prevents SQL injection but does NOT neutralize LIKE-pattern semantics — the metacharacters are interpreted by the LIKE engine. The injection_security_test.go only asserts SQL-comment/UNION passthrough; it never exercises LIKE wildcard semantics, so this property is uncovered.
findings.proposedFix: Escape LIKE metacharacters in f.Value before wrapping: replace \\ -> \\\\, % -> \\%, _ -> \\_ (engine-appropriate escape char) and append an explicit `ESCAPE '\\'` to the LIKE fragment, in both ApplyToQuery and ApplyToCountQuery so count and data agree.

verdict.reason: The cited code is exactly as described: filter.go:194 and filter.go:216 build `f.Field+" LIKE $1"` with arg `"%"+f.Value+"%"`, where f.Value flows raw from r.URL.Query() (attacker-controlled), with no LIKE-metacharacter escaping and no ESCAPE clause. The doc-comment (line 53) documents _like as "(contains)", and injection_security_test.go only asserts SQL-comment/UNION passthrough, never LIKE wildcard semantics — so the property is genuinely uncovered and triggerable (`?field_like=%25` yields `LIKE '%%%'`). However the security severity is overstated. (1) No authz bypass: _like is an additive filter applied after owner/tenant scoping in CRUD, so broadening the match only returns rows the caller is already authorized to see — it does not "leak rows the filter was meant to exclude" across any trust boundary. (2) The DoS angle is no worse than baseline: every _like query already has a leading `%`, so it is non-indexable and full-scans regardless of attacker metacharacters; underscores/percent do not change the cost class. So this is a real correctness/robustness quirk (substring contract not enforced) worth a P3 hardening, not a P2 vuln. Refined down rather than refuted as false.

findings.property: Search cost must be bounded by a cap on the attacker-controlled query, not multiplied by both query-term count and corpus size.
findings.title: Unbounded query-term count amplifies search cost (DoS)
findings.attack: Attacker hits any handler that forwards a user query string into search.Query.Text (e.g. ?q=...). Send a query of, say, 1 MB of distinct/whitespace-separated tokens. strings.Fields splits it into ~100k+ terms with no cap or dedup; scoreTerms then runs strings.Count(haystack, term) once per term for EVERY document under the RLock. Cost is O(terms x corpus x doclen) per request — a single small HTTP request forces hundreds of millions of substring scans, pinning CPU and holding the read lock.
findings.evidence: memory.go:42 `terms := strings.Fields(strings.ToLower(query.Text))` has no length/term-count limit and no dedup. scoreTerms (lines 72-85) loops over every term calling `strings.Count(haystack, term)`, and Memory.Search (lines 44-54) loops that over every document. There is no MaxQueryLen / MaxTerms guard anywhere in the package (grep shows none). The only callers cap Limit, never the query size, so the work is done before Limit is applied.
findings.proposedFix: In Search, bound the query before scanning: cap len(query.Text) (e.g. reject or truncate > a few KB), cap len(terms) (e.g. first N terms), and dedup terms (a term repeated K times multiplies cost K-fold but adds no selectivity). Optionally return an error for over-long queries so callers can 400 rather than silently truncate.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code: battery/search/memory.go:42 `terms := strings.Fields(strings.ToLower(query.Text))` has no length/term-count cap and no dedup; scoreTerms (lines 77-82) loops `strings.Count(haystack, term)` once per term, and Memory.Search (lines 44-54) repeats that per document, so cost is O(terms x corpus x doclen). Limit is only applied after the full scan (lines 65-69), so it does not bound the work. A 1MB query splits into ~100k+ terms with no guard. The defect is attacker-reachable: examples/blog/main.go:58-72 forwards `?q=` straight into Query.Text with only Limit:10. No MaxTerms/MaxQueryLen guard exists in the package, and there is no *_security_test.go in battery/search asserting this property, so it is not already covered. I could not refute it. Two caveats lower severity to P3: (1) Memory carries the doc-comment "an in-memory search backend suitable for tests and small examples" (memory.go:10) which scopes it as a perf disclaimer, though it does not document attacker-query amplification as acceptable, so it is not a true escape-hatch contradiction; (2) the only shipped attacker surface is an example app, not framework HTTP code — the production-grade path is battery/embed. The amplification mechanism is genuine, but realistic blast radius depends on a host deploying Memory in production despite the disclaimer.

findings.property: An integer that exceeds a field's Max (or is below Min) must be rejected regardless of magnitude.
findings.title: Int Min/Max check via float64 loses precision, bypassing large bounds
findings.attack: For an Int field with a large bound such as Max=1e18, submit the value as a JSON string "1000000000000000001" (or any int64 > the bound but within ~1 ULP of float64 at that magnitude). toInt64 parses it exactly, but validateInt compares float64(n) > *f.Max where both operands round to the same float64, so the comparison is false and the over-limit value is accepted.
findings.evidence: validateInt (validate.go:87-92) does `float64(n) < *f.Min` and `float64(n) > *f.Max`. int64 values above 2^53 are not exactly representable as float64, so float64(MaxInt64-100) == float64(MaxInt64). Verified: float64(9223372036854775807) > float64(9223372036854775806) is false. The attacker controls n (parsed exactly from the request as int64/string); the precision loss is entirely on the comparison side, so a value strictly greater than the configured Max passes validation. Distinct from the already-reported float->int64 saturation finding (there the *input* is a float being truncated; here the input is an exact int64 and the *bound comparison* is lossy).
findings.proposedFix: Compare integer bounds in integer space: convert *f.Min/*f.Max to int64 once (with range guards) and compare n against them as int64, or use math/big, instead of widening n to float64. Reserve the float64 comparison for the Float/Decimal types only.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in core/schema/validate.go:87-92: validateInt does `float64(n) < *f.Min` and `float64(n) > *f.Max`. toInt64 (lines 287-332) parses a JSON string like "1000000000000000001" exactly into an int64, but widening to float64 for the comparison loses precision above 2^53. I verified at runtime that for Max=1e18 (exactly representable) and n=1000000000000000001, `n > 1e18` is true yet `float64(n) > 1e18` is false (the two round to the same float64), so the over-limit value is accepted. The attacker controls `value` (parsed exactly), and the precision loss is on the comparison of that attacker input, so it is correctly attacker input, not config. No *_security_test.go exists in core/schema and the only Int bound test (TestValidateInt_MinMax, validate_test.go:123) uses bounds 0/150, so the boundary is uncovered. Distinct from any float->int64 saturation finding (there the input float is truncated; here the input int64 is exact and the bound comparison is lossy). Severity stays P3 because it only triggers for bounds at extreme magnitudes (>=~9e15), and Min/Max are themselves *float64 so integer bounds above 2^53 cannot even be expressed precisely by design — but the bug still admits values strictly greater than an exactly-representable Max (1e18 accepts 1e18+1).

findings.property: A sanitized filename must not contain any character that a downstream log writer / terminal / JS context treats as a line terminator (the doc-comment promises a logged filename 'can't escape its log line via injected newlines').
findings.title: Unicode line separators (U+2028/U+2029/U+0085) survive sanitization
findings.attack: Upload with a multipart filename containing U+0085 (NEL, bytes C2 85) or U+2028/U+2029 (LINE SEPARATOR, bytes E2 80 A8/A9), e.g. filename "a injected.jpg". These are >0x20 per byte so they pass stripControlBytes, and the metadata OriginalName/Key are later logged.
findings.evidence: Lines 173-184: stripControlBytes only drops bytes `c < 0x20 || c == 0x7f`, i.e. ASCII control bytes. The multibyte UTF-8 encodings of U+0085, U+2028, U+2029 each have only continuation/lead bytes >= 0x80, so none are removed. Verified: SanitizeFilename("a<U+2028>b<U+0085>c.jpg") returns the string with e280a8 and c285 intact. U+2028/U+2029 are line terminators in JavaScript/JSON-adjacent tooling and U+0085 is NEL in many terminals and log processors, defeating the documented newline-injection defense for any consumer that splits on Unicode newlines.
findings.proposedFix: After stripControlBytes, also strip or replace the Unicode line/paragraph separators and NEL: drop runes where unicode.IsControl(r) || r == '' || r == ' ' || r == ' ', iterating by rune rather than byte.

verdict.reason: Verified empirically: SanitizeFilename("a b cd.jpg") returns bytes "...e2 80 a8 ... e2 80 a9 ... c2 85 ..." intact — stripControlBytes (validate.go:173-184) only drops bytes c<0x20||c==0x7f, so the multibyte UTF-8 encodings of U+2028/U+2029/U+0085 (all bytes >=0x80) survive. The sanitized result becomes the storage Key (upload.go:109, filefield.go:316). No *_security_test.go asserts the Unicode-separator property (the hardening test at upload_hardening_security_test.go:22-23 only covers ASCII \r/\n), so it is not already covered. Attacker controls the multipart filename, so this is the right layer. It does NOT contradict the doc-comment: the comment (validate.go:89-99) scopes its promise to "injected newlines or terminal control sequences" / "CR/LF" — the named ASCII threats — and those realistic consumers (Go slog/log, HTTP Content-Disposition) do not split on these runes. Additionally the metadata is emitted via json.NewEncoder which already escapes U+2028/U+2029 to  /  (Go's built-in JS-injection defense); only U+0085 leaks literally into JSON. Net: a genuine, triggerable gap but a marginal P3 hardening item, not a clear contract violation, since the documented defense targets ASCII CR/LF and the main JS/JSON consumer is already protected.

findings.property: An attachment filename must not be able to break out of the quoted MIME name/filename parameter and inject additional header parameters.
findings.title: Unescaped double-quote in attachment filename injects MIME params
