findings.attack: Send an email with Attachment.Filename = `report.csv"; name="invoice.pdf` (or `x"; filename="setup.exe`). No CR/LF/NUL, so assertNoHeaderInjection passes. buildMessage emits `Content-Type: text/csv; name="report.csv"; name="invoice.pdf"` and `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="report.csv"; name="invoice.pdf"`. The injected closing quote terminates the quoted parameter early and appends attacker-chosen parameters, enabling attachment-name spoofing (display name differs from the dangerous real name) and duplicate/conflicting MIME parameters.
findings.evidence: smtp.go:234 and 236 interpolate att.Filename directly inside double-quoted parameters (`name=\"" + att.Filename + "\"`) with no quoting/escaping. assertNoHeaderInjection (smtp.go:272-278) only rejects \r \n \x00 — a bare `\"` is allowed. The sibling email_security_test.go TestEmail_AttachmentFilenameHeaderInjection only exercises a filename that ALSO contains \r\n, so it passes via the CRLF check and never asserts the pure-double-quote breakout; no test feeds a quote-only filename.
findings.proposedFix: Properly encode the filename for the MIME parameter instead of raw string concatenation: use mime.FormatMediaType / a quoted-string escaper that backslash-escapes embedded `"` and `\`, or RFC 2231 ext-value encoding (filename*=). Equivalently, reject or escape `"` (and `\`) in att.Filename in assertNoHeaderInjection's filename path. Apply the same encoding to att.ContentType which has the identical breakout via `; param=`.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code. smtp.go:234 and 236 concatenate att.Filename raw inside double-quoted MIME params (`name=\"" + att.Filename + "\"` and `filename=\"" + att.Filename + "\"`) with no quoted-string escaping. The guard at smtp.go:177-183 calls assertNoHeaderInjection, which (smtp.go:272-278) rejects only CR/LF/NUL — a bare `"` passes. So Filename=`report.csv"; name="invoice.pdf` produces `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="report.csv"; name="invoice.pdf"`, a genuine within-line MIME-parameter breakout (same applies to ContentType at line 234 via `; param=`). The sibling tests TestEmail_AttachmentFilenameHeaderInjection (line 97) and TestEmail_AttachmentContentTypeHeaderInjection (line 116) both embed `\r\n`, so they assert only the CRLF path and never the quote-only breakout — property not covered. NOT wrongLayer: attachment filenames are commonly user-controlled (uploaded files re-emailed). I downgrade to P3, not the finder's P2: CR/LF is already blocked so no actual SMTP header injection / new-header smuggling is possible — the impact is limited to attachment-name spoofing and duplicate/conflicting MIME parameters within a single header line, not auth bypass or new-header injection. Fix is sound: use mime.FormatMediaType or backslash-escape `"`/`\` (or reject them) for both Filename and ContentType.

findings.title: html-mode signal injects unescaped JSON.stringify of error object
findings.property: HTML-mode signal rendering must not route an automatically-constructed error object's untrusted server-error body into live markup.
findings.attack: A signal bound with data-fui-signal-mode=html is also used as a dispatchRPC response signal. On a non-ok response, dispatchRPC calls setSignal(responseSignal, { ok:false, status, text: txt }) where txt is the server's raw error body (commonly echoing attacker-supplied input, e.g. a validation message containing the submitted value). In html mode the value is an object, so line 831 runs node.innerHTML = JSON.stringify(value). JSON.stringify does not HTML-escape, so inside text is injected verbatim and executes.
findings.evidence: Line 831: node.innerHTML = (typeof value === 'string') ? value : (value == null ? '' : JSON.stringify(value)); the non-string branch injects JSON.stringify output as markup. dispatchRPC line 159 (and widgets.js:341) feed an object whose text field is await r.text() of a failure response. html mode is a documented escape hatch for trusted HTML strings; the surprising path is the object/error case silently routing untrusted server-error text through innerHTML, so flagging rather than asserting against the doc.
findings.proposedFix: In the non-string html-mode branch, do not inject JSON.stringify output as markup: set textContent for non-string values, or only honor html mode when typeof value === 'string' and use textContent otherwise (mirroring the text-mode behavior at line 856).

verdict.reason: Verified as a real, triggerable XSS. Sink confirmed at core-ui/runtime/runtime.js:831 — in html mode a non-string value is rendered as `node.innerHTML = JSON.stringify(value)`, which does NOT HTML-escape, so `` inside a string field parses and executes. Feeder confirmed at core-ui/runtime/src/widgets.js:340-341: on a non-ok RPC the handler does `NS.setSignal(responseSignal, { ok:false, status, text:txt })` where `txt = await r.text()` is the raw server error body (commonly echoes submitted input). widgets.js NS is `window.__gofastr` (line 27), the same setSignal, so the cross-file path is genuine. core-ui/ARCHITECTURE.md:216-220 documents the exact intended pairing of `data-fui-rpc-signal` with `data-fui-signal-mode=html`, so binding both on one signal is the sanctioned pattern, not misuse — the error-object branch silently diverges. Not asserted in runtime_security_test.go (only covers attr-mode scheme guard and CSRF). Not wrongLayer: in text mode the identical object renders harmlessly via textContent (line 856), so html mode is uniquely unsafe for the auto-built error object; the attacker-controlled surface is the reflected server error body, a legitimate web sink. Not a contract contradiction: the html escape hatch is for trusted HTML *strings* (success path returns a string body); the proposed fix (use textContent for non-string values) preserves all documented string behavior while closing the object path. P3 is fair: requires a server error body that reflects attacker input plus the html-mode binding.

findings.property: Shared mutable fields must be accessed under the same lock by all readers and writers.
findings.title: Data race on lc.timeout between Shutdown and SetShutdownTimeout
findings.attack: Shutdown reads lc.timeout without holding lc.mu (line 156: context.WithTimeout(ctx, lc.timeout)), while SetShutdownTimeout writes it under lc.mu (line 240-243). A concurrent SetShutdownTimeout during Shutdown is an unsynchronized read/write. Confirmed: `go test -race` reports WARNING: DATA RACE on a goroutine calling SetShutdownTimeout concurrently with Shutdown.
findings.evidence: lifecycle.go:156 reads lc.timeout outside the mutex; lifecycle.go:241-242 writes it inside the mutex. Reproduced a DATA RACE under -race with two concurrent goroutines.
findings.proposedFix: Read the timeout under the lock at the start of Shutdown: snapshot `lc.mu.Lock(); to := lc.timeout; lc.mu.Unlock()` before context.WithTimeout, or store timeout as an atomic.Int64 of nanoseconds. WithTimeout option (line 83) also writes timeout without a lock during New, which is fine, but the runtime SetShutdownTimeout path needs the synchronized read.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code: Shutdown reads lc.timeout outside the mutex at lifecycle.go:156 (context.WithTimeout(ctx, lc.timeout)), while SetShutdownTimeout writes it under lc.mu at lifecycle.go:240-242. I reproduced WARNING: DATA RACE under `go test -race` with concurrent goroutines (read at lifecycle.go:156 vs write at lifecycle.go:242). Every other shared field (drainers, checkers) is read+written under lc.mu, so timeout is the lone field violating the package's own locking discipline. No *_security_test.go exists in the package and lifecycle_test.go only calls SetShutdownTimeout sequentially in setup, so the property is not asserted. Lifecycle is exposed publicly via App.Lifecycle(), so a host could call SetShutdownTimeout concurrently with a signal-driven Shutdown, making the race triggerable — though no first-party code calls SetShutdownTimeout outside tests, so this is a genuine but low-stakes correctness bug (P3), not a security issue or attacker-triggerable defect.

findings.property: String Min/Max length constraints should bound the number of characters, not the UTF-8 byte count.
findings.title: String length uses byte count, not rune count
findings.attack: A field with Min: 5 intended as a minimum-character control (e.g. a code/username) accepts a 2-character multibyte string such as "👍👍" (8 bytes) because float64(len(s))=8 >= 5. Conversely Max: 3 rejects the legitimate 4-character "日本語あ". Verified at runtime: validateField(String,Min:5,"👍👍")=nil (2 chars passes a 5-char minimum).
findings.evidence: validate.go:62 and 65 use `float64(len(s))` which is the byte length, while the error messages (lines 63,66) say "characters". For ASCII this matches, but multibyte input lets a too-short string satisfy Min, weakening any minimum-length validation. JSONSchema emits the same numbers as minLength/maxLength (jsonschema.go:39-43) which are spec'd in code points, so the generated schema and the server validator disagree for non-ASCII. No rune/byte test exists in validate_test.go.
findings.proposedFix: Use `utf8.RuneCountInString(s)` instead of `len(s)` for the Min/Max comparisons in validateString (and validateDecimal is unaffected). This makes the runtime check match the "characters" wording and the generated JSON Schema semantics.

verdict.reason: Verified in core/schema/validate.go:62 and :65: validateString uses float64(len(s)) (UTF-8 byte length) while the error messages on lines 63/66 say "characters", and jsonschema.go:39-43,52-55 emit the same Min/Max as minLength/maxLength which JSON Schema defines in Unicode code points. So a 2-rune string like "👍👍" (8 bytes) passes Min:5 and a 4-rune string like "日本語あ" (12 bytes) is rejected by Max:3 — a real runtime/schema disagreement for non-ASCII input. validateField is the live validation path (called from schema.go:123,151,178). No *_security_test.go exists in the package and validate_test.go has no rune/byte assertion, so it is uncovered. It is a genuine but minor correctness/semantics bug, not an auth/injection issue, so the finder's bug/P3 classification is correct; the proposed utf8.RuneCountInString fix contradicts no documented escape hatch (the "characters" wording confirms intent). Mild caveat: minimum-length alone is rarely a load-bearing security control, so impact is low.

findings.property: Params(r) must return the value of every path parameter declared in the route pattern, including a catch-all {name...} wildcard.
findings.title: Params() drops catch-all {name...} segment value
findings.attack: Register r.Get("/files/{path...}", h) and request /files/a/b/c. A handler that reads the captured path from Params(req)["path"] gets a missing key (empty string).
findings.evidence: Params (router.go:139) extracts the name as pattern[i+1:i+end], which for `{path...}` yields the literal `path...` (including the dots), then calls r.PathValue("path..."). Go's ServeMux stores the catch-all under key `path`, not `path...`, so PathValue returns "" and the `if val != ""` guard omits it entirely. I confirmed with a throwaway test: Params() returned an empty map for /files/a/b/c while Param(req,"path") returned "a/b/c". Handlers that drive logic (including authorization or file-path resolution) off Params() instead of the per-name Param() silently see no value, which can fail-open (empty path treated as root/allow).
findings.proposedFix: Strip a trailing `...` (and handle the special `{$}` token) when deriving the param name before calling r.PathValue, i.e. name := strings.TrimSuffix(pattern[i+1:i+end], "..."). Add a test asserting Params() includes the catch-all value.

verdict.reason: Empirically confirmed: for r.Get("/files/{path...}",...) requesting /files/a/b/c, Params(req) returns an empty map while Param(req,"path") returns "a/b/c". router.go:139 extracts name := pattern[i+1:i+end] which for "{path...}" yields the literal "path..."; r.PathValue("path...") returns "" (ServeMux stores it under "path"), and the val != "" guard at line 141 omits it. This violates the doc-comment contract ("extracts all path parameters", lines 120-122). Not covered by sibling tests: router_security_test.go only asserts catch-all routing isolation (TestRouter_CatchAllDoesNotLeak) and newline/NUL stripping; TestWildcardParam tests Param() singular, not Params(). However the security framing (fail-open auth/path resolution) is speculative — there are ZERO non-test callers of router.Params in the repo, and the core-ui app Resolve uses its own param matching, not router.Params. So it is a genuine correctness/contract bug in a public helper but with no current dangerous consumer, hence P3 rather than P2. Proposed fix (TrimSuffix "...") is correct and contradicts no escape hatch.

findings.property: Rendering a bounded markdown input must not consume time superlinear in the input size.
findings.title: Quadratic blowup on unmatched emphasis delimiters
findings.attack: A document body consisting of many unmatched emphasis delimiters, e.g. `____…____x` (N underscores). Each delimiter run triggers a full findClosingDelim scan to end-of-input that finds no match, giving O(N^2) work.
findings.evidence: renderInline, on hitting '*'/'_', calls findClosingDelim(input, i+run, …) which linearly scans the remaining input; when there is no closing run it scans to the end and returns -1, then renderInline advances by one and repeats for the next delimiter. Measured: N=5000 -> 7ms, N=20000 -> 105ms, N=50000 -> 657ms (clean quadratic). A ~200KB body of underscores costs ~10s of CPU. RenderHTML is an exported API and may be fed untrusted markdown (comments, user docs), making this a single-request CPU-DoS amplifier.
findings.proposedFix: Cap the scan distance or total inline work, or pre-index delimiter positions so an unmatched opener is not re-scanned for every subsequent delimiter. At minimum, bound input length for RenderHTML on untrusted paths, or memoize 'no closing delim found from position p for run r'.

verdict.reason: Confirmed and reproduced. inline.go:58-81: on each '*'/'_', renderInline calls findClosingDelim(input, i+run, ...) (line 60), which (lines 112-135) linearly scans to end-of-input and returns -1 when no matching run exists; renderInline then advances by one byte (line 81) and repeats for every subsequent delimiter, yielding O(N^2). scanRun caps run length at 3 (lines 103-105) but does not bound the re-scan. There is no input-length guard anywhere in RenderHTML/Render/renderBody (markdown.go) or renderInline. Measured on the current code: N=50000 -> 688ms, N=100000 -> 2.85s, N=200000 -> 11.3s (clean quadratic), so a ~200KB underscore body burns ~10s CPU per request. RenderHTML is exported and reachable via framework/ui.Markdown (framework/ui/markdown.go:49), a generic component a host can feed user-supplied markdown. The sibling xss_security_test.go asserts only XSS properties (no perf/DoS/superlinear test). doc.go frames the package for "inputs we actually write" but states no escape hatch forbidding untrusted input, so a fix (length cap or memoizing no-close-found) contradicts no documented contract. Severity P3 is appropriate: single-request CPU amplifier, only triggerable if a host wires untrusted content through this renderer.

findings.property: HTTP method written to structured logs must be control-character-sanitized to prevent log-line forgery, consistently across every logging entry point.
findings.title: SampledLogging logs raw r.Method (log injection)
findings.attack: Reach a route through the SampledLogging/SampledLoggingFn middleware (documented as the recommended production logger) with an r.Method containing CR/LF or terminal-escape bytes — reachable via an X-HTTP-Method-Override style middleware that rewrites r.Method, or any non-stdlib/proxied request source. The forged bytes land verbatim in the operator's log stream, letting an attacker inject a fake log line / terminal-control payload.
findings.evidence: LoggingFn (line 38-42) defensively passes `safeLogMethod(r.Method)`, and the package ships TestLogging_SanitizesMethod asserting exactly that property. But SampledLoggingFn logs `"method", r.Method` raw in both branches (lines 110-116 and 122-130) while still sanitizing the path via safeLogPath. The method sanitization is inconsistently applied to the entry point the doc-comment recommends for production. No sampled-logging test covers method sanitization (only TestSampledLoggingUsesInjectedLogger exists).
findings.proposedFix: Wrap r.Method in safeLogMethod() in both log calls inside SampledLoggingFn, matching LoggingFn.

verdict.reason: Code reading is accurate: in /Users/dom/programming/gofastr/.claude/worktrees/iterative-chasing-wreath/core/middleware/logging.go, SampledLoggingFn logs `"method", r.Method` raw at lines 111 and 124, while LoggingFn sanitizes it via `safeLogMethod(r.Method)` at line 38; the path is sanitized via safeLogPath in all paths. The package's own threat model treats CRLF/ESC in r.Method as a real attack — safeLogMethod's doc-comment (lines 135-138) says it exists so "an attacker who got a CRLF / ESC sequence into r.Method can't forge a fake log entry," and TestLogging_SanitizesMethod asserts exactly this. That security test only exercises LoggingWithWriter (the LoggingFn path); it does NOT cover SampledLoggingFn, so the method-sanitization property is genuinely unasserted on the sampled path, which the doc (line 75) recommends as the production default. The inconsistency is therefore real and not already covered. Severity stays P3, and confidence is only medium on triggerability: Go's stdlib server rejects control bytes in the method token at parse time, and grep shows no X-HTTP-Method-Override / r.Method-rewriting middleware anywhere in the repo (only test code assigns r.Method), so a raw-network attacker cannot reach it through the standard server — but since the maintainers already shipped a defensive guard for this exact property and dropped it on the recommended-production path, refuting it outright would contradict the package's own established contract.

findings.property: Stop() must return rather than block indefinitely when the scheduler was never started.
findings.title: Scheduler.Stop() deadlocks if Start() never called
findings.attack: Host code (or a shutdown path that runs before startup completes) calls scheduler.Stop() without ever having called Start() — e.g. an early error aborts boot and the defer Stop() fires. The goroutine blocks forever, hanging graceful shutdown.
findings.evidence: Stop() (cron.go:103-111) closes s.stop then blocks on `<-s.stopped`. s.stopped is only closed by `defer close(s.stopped)` inside run() (cron.go:146), and run() is launched exclusively by Start() via startOnce. If Start() was never invoked, run() never executes, so s.stopped is never closed and the `<-s.stopped` receive blocks forever. Reproduced: a goroutine calling Stop() on a freshly NewScheduler() never returns within 2s. The existing cron_test.go only covers Stop after Start and double-Stop, not Stop-before-Start.
findings.proposedFix: Track started state (e.g. a `started` flag set inside startOnce.Do, guarded by mu, or use a `started chan struct{}` closed in Start). In Stop(), if the scheduler was never started, close s.stopped (or simply return) instead of blocking on `<-s.stopped`.

verdict.reason: Confirmed by reproduction: Stop() (cron.go:103-111) closes s.stop then blocks on bare `<-s.stopped`; s.stopped is closed only by `defer close(s.stopped)` in run() (cron.go:146), which is launched solely by Start() via startOnce (cron.go:96-98). With no Start, run() never executes so Stop() blocks forever (test deadlocked at 1.5s). This is reachable in framework code: App.AddCron (app.go:891-900) registers s.Stop as an OnStop drainer; if an earlier OnStart hook fails, runStartHooks (app.go:977-981) aborts before s.Start, then abort()->Shutdown()->lc.Shutdown() invokes drainers synchronously (lifecycle.go:179-190) and the 5s drainCtx (lifecycle.go:156) cannot interrupt the context-unaware channel receive, hanging graceful shutdown. Neither cron_security_test.go nor cron_test.go (only Stop-after-Start and double-Stop) assert this property. No documented escape hatch contradicted.

findings.property: A documented visibility timeout must actually re-deliver jobs abandoned in the processing set.
findings.title: Redis visibility timeout is recorded but never enforced (lost jobs)
findings.attack: A worker crashes (or its process is killed) after Dequeue moves a job into the processing hash but before Ack/Nack. The job sits in <queue>:processing forever; no consumer ever sees it again.
findings.evidence: redis.go:105-109 writes an `expiresAt` field into the processing hash, but grep shows expiresAt and visibilityTimeout are never read by any reclaim/reaper code path — there is no method that scans :processing for expired entries and re-LPushes them. The type comment (redis.go:25-26) and agents.md promise 'a visibility timeout for in-flight jobs', so abandoned jobs are silently and permanently lost, defeating the durability guarantee.
findings.proposedFix: Add a Reclaim(ctx) method (and document calling it periodically, or run it from a background goroutine) that iterates the processing hash, finds entries whose expiresAt < now, re-enqueues the underlying job onto the main list, and HDels the processing entry.

verdict.reason: Verified: redis.go:107 writes an `expiresAt` field into the `:processing` hash, but grep across the whole package shows `expiresAt`/`visibilityTimeout` are only ever written, never read — there is no reaper/reclaim method, and RedisQueue does not implement any re-delivery path. A worker that crashes after Dequeue (line 109 HSet) but before Ack/Nack leaves the job in `<queue>:processing` permanently, contradicting the doc-comment at redis.go:46-47 ("considered abandoned and eligible for re-delivery") and the type comment at lines 24-26 ("supports a visibility timeout for in-flight jobs"). No `*_security_test.go` exists and queue_test.go only asserts processing-hash membership after Ack (lines 431-442), never timeout reclaim. However this is a functional/durability bug, NOT a security defect: the trigger is an operational worker crash, not attacker-controlled input, so it does not meet the security bar that defines a finding here. Severity is further softened because agents.md steers all real workloads to DBQueue (documented as "lease-based worker claim (safe under crashes)") and never recommends RedisQueue for durability — RedisQueue's unfulfilled promise is a code-comment/contract gap rather than an exploitable vulnerability. Refined to P3.

findings.property: Strict-key body validation must accept JSON keys for fields promoted from embedded structs, since encoding/json accepts them.
findings.title: Bind rejects valid JSON keys from embedded struct fields
findings.attack: Any handler whose bind input struct embeds another struct (a common Go pattern, e.g. `type Req struct { CommonFields; Extra int }`) rejects ALL valid requests: a client POSTing the promoted field (`{"name":"alice","age":30}` where `name` is declared on the embedded struct) gets HTTP 400 `invalid JSON: unknown field "name"`. This is a functional DoS for those endpoints.
findings.evidence: jsonTagSet (bind.go:183-201) iterates only `t.NumField()` and never recurses into anonymous/embedded struct fields, so promoted JSON keys are absent from `allowed`. validateBodyKeys (bind.go:169-171) then rejects the promoted key as 'unknown field' BEFORE the stdlib decoder (which would have accepted it via DisallowUnknownFields) ever runs. I confirmed with a live test using an embedded struct: `Bind` returned `invalid JSON: unknown field "name"` on a fully valid body. The sibling bind_strict_keys_security_test.go has no embedded-struct case, so this is uncovered.
findings.proposedFix: In jsonTagSet, when a field has `f.Anonymous` and its type (deref pointer) is a struct with no explicit json tag, recurse and merge the embedded type's tag set (matching encoding/json's promotion rules). Fields with an explicit json tag on the embedded field are named normally. This keeps the strict-key protection while not false-rejecting promoted fields.

verdict.reason: Confirmed via live test: a body {"name":"alice","age":30} bound into a struct embedding another struct (name promoted from the embedded type) returns HTTP 400 `invalid JSON: unknown field "name"`. jsonTagSet (bind.go:183-201) only iterates t.NumField() and never recurses into f.Anonymous embedded struct fields, so promoted JSON keys are absent from `allowed`; validateBodyKeys (bind.go:169-171) rejects them before the DisallowUnknownFields decoder (which would accept them) runs. No embedded-struct case exists in bind_strict_keys_security_test.go. No doc comment carves out embedded fields, so the fix does not contradict a documented contract. Severity refined to P3: the bug fails closed (over-rejects, no security bypass), requires the developer to use struct embedding (a developer-side shape, not attacker input), and no current framework Bind caller uses embedding — so it is a correctness/availability defect for host-app endpoints rather than a triggerable security hole.

findings.property: A Decimal value (stored verbatim as a string) must only be accepted if it is a canonical decimal numeric representation.
findings.title: validateDecimal accepts non-decimal float literal forms
findings.attack: Submit a Decimal field value such as "1_000", "0x1p4", "+5", ".5", "5.", or "-Inf". validateDecimal accepts it via strconv.ParseFloat and the raw string is stored in the decimal/numeric column.
findings.evidence: validate.go:125 validates Decimal with strconv.ParseFloat(s,64), which accepts Go float-literal forms that are NOT valid decimal text: "1_000" (underscore separators), "0x1p4" (hex float), "+5", ".5", "5.", and "Inf"/"-Inf". schema.go:11 states Decimal is 'stored as string', so the un-normalized string is persisted verbatim while the bound check runs on the parsed float. A downstream consumer or numeric DB column then receives a value it cannot reparse as intended (e.g. "1_000" or "0x1p4"), corrupting the field. Probe confirmed "1_000", "0x1p4", "+5", ".5", "5.", "1e3" all returned nil.
findings.proposedFix: Before bound validation, require a strict decimal form (e.g. regex ^[+-]?(\d+(\.\d*)?|\.\d+)$, rejecting underscore/hex/Inf/NaN/exponent unless explicitly intended), then parse the matched canonical form for Min/Max so the stored string stays a valid decimal representation.

verdict.reason: validate.go:125 uses strconv.ParseFloat(s,64), which I confirmed by probe accepts "1_000", "0x1p4", "+5", ".5", "5.", "1e3", "Inf", "-Inf", "NaN"; the package has no *_security_test.go and validate_test.go only checks "99.99"/"notanumber", so the strict-decimal property is genuinely unasserted and the input flows verbatim (jsonschema.go:80 format:decimal, diff.go:38 maps to Postgres DECIMAL). However the candidate's "corrupting the field" / P2 claim is overstated: Postgres DECIMAL rejects "0x1p4"/"1_000"/"Inf"/"-Inf"/"NaN" at INSERT (a failed write, not silent corruption) and accepts "+5"/".5"/"5."/"1e3" as valid numerics that round-trip correctly. The value is bound as a parameter, not interpolated, so there is no injection or attacker-controlled corruption. Real but a P3 lax-validation/robustness gap (validation passes strings the storage layer later rejects), not the claimed P2 corruption bug.

findings.property: A request to an existing path with an unregistered method must return 405 Method Not Allowed, not be masked as 404.
findings.title: Custom NotFound swallows native 405, returns 404
findings.attack: Register `GET /u/{id}` and a custom NotFound handler (typical in production). Send `POST /u/1`. Go's ServeMux returns an empty pattern for a method mismatch (the 405 path), so ServeHTTP treats it as not-found and runs the custom NotFound handler — the response becomes 404 with no `Allow` header, instead of the native `405 Method Not Allowed, Allow: GET, HEAD`. Confirmed: with NotFound set the response was `code=404 body="nf"`; without it, `code=405 allow="GET, HEAD"`.
findings.evidence: router.go:314 `_, pattern := r.mux.Handler(req)`; line 316 treats `pattern == ""` as not-found and dispatches effectiveNotFound. Go's ServeMux returns pattern=="" for BOTH a true 404 and a 405 method mismatch, so the router cannot distinguish them and the custom NotFound overrides the native 405. The doc-comment at lines 303-307 explicitly claims 'Go 1.22+ ServeMux handles 405 ... natively', but that only holds when no custom NotFound is set. The sibling security test TestRouter_MethodNotAllowed runs WITHOUT a custom NotFound, so this regression is uncovered.
findings.proposedFix: In ServeHTTP, before invoking effectiveNotFound, detect the 405 case: when pattern=="" run the request through a throwaway recorder via r.mux.ServeHTTP (or inspect mux for a same-path different-method match) and only fall back to the custom NotFound for a genuine 404; otherwise let the native 405 (with Allow header) stand. Alternatively probe r.mux.Handler with a synthetic GET request to see if the path exists under another method.

verdict.reason: Verified by direct reproduction against the real Router. router.go:314 `_, pattern := r.mux.Handler(req)`; Go's ServeMux returns pattern=="" for BOTH a true 404 and a 405 method mismatch (confirmed: POST to GET-only /u/1 -> pattern==""). Lines 316-320 treat pattern=="" as not-found, so when a custom NotFound is set the router discards the mux's native 405 handler and runs effectiveNotFound, yielding code=404 body="nf" with no Allow header; without the custom NotFound the same request yields code=405 Allow="GET, HEAD". Not covered: TestRouter_MethodNotAllowed (line 12) runs with NO custom NotFound, and TestRouter_NotFoundCustom (line 107) only exercises a genuine 404 (/nonexistent), so the combination is uncovered. Not wrong-layer: the route+NotFound are dev config but the trigger is the attacker-controlled request method on an existing path. No contract contradiction: the doc-comment at 303-307 asserts 405 works natively (which is broken here, not documented as intentional). Refined to P3 because the wrong-method request still does NOT reach the GET handler (returns 404, not 200) — no auth/handler bypass or info leak; impact is HTTP correctness (lost 405/Allow semantics, RFC 7231), not a security bypass.

findings.title: SSE island name interpolated into CSS selector unescaped
findings.property: An island update must write its HTML only into the element it names, never an attacker-chosen element selected via injected CSS-selector syntax.
findings.attack: In an app whose SSE island names derive from user-influenced data, a crafted island name like x"], [data-some-trusted-region makes the selector resolve to a DIFFERENT unintended element, and el.innerHTML = html overwrites that region with the frame's html. A name like x"] yields an invalid selector that throws (silently swallowed), denying the legitimate island its update.
findings.evidence: Lines 26-30: const { island, html } = JSON.parse(event.data); ... document.querySelector(`[data-island="${island}"]`); ... el.innerHTML = html; The island field from the SSE JSON is interpolated into a CSS attribute selector with no CSS.escape() and no validation, unlike widgets.js which uses CSS.escape() for analogous lookups (widgets.js:55,142). runtime_test.go TestRuntimeModule_SSE asserts only that data-island appears; nothing pins escaping of the island key.
findings.proposedFix: Escape the dynamic key: document.querySelector('[data-island="' + CSS.escape(String(island)) + '"]'), matching the CSS.escape pattern already used in widgets.js/toasts.js; optionally restrict island to a [\w.-]+ charset.

verdict.reason: Verified at core-ui/runtime/src/sse.js:28: `document.querySelector(`[data-island="${island}"]`)` interpolates the SSE `island` field raw into a CSS attribute selector with no CSS.escape() — while the sibling widgets.js escapes analogous data-* lookups (CSS.escape at lines 55,142). The `island` value derives from server-side Island.ID (NewIsland id, island.go:21), and the package's OWN security tests (island_security_test.go TestSecurity_IslandID_HTMLInjection/AttributeInjection) treat hostile island IDs as in-scope, so the precondition is consistent with the project threat model, not pure developer config. No runtime_security_test.go test pins SSE-selector escaping. The defect is genuine and uncovered, but its impact is narrower than claimed: el.innerHTML = isl.Update() writes the server's OWN HTML-escaped island content (island.go:51), not attacker markup, so a crafted selector causes wrong-region-gets-this-island's-HTML or a silently-swallowed invalid-selector throw (lost update) — a robustness/correctness glitch, not content injection. P3 is appropriate.

findings.property: A live SSE update stream for a session must stay alive as long as at least one connection for that session is open.
findings.title: Second SSE connection per session tears down the first on disconnect
findings.attack: A user opens the same page (same session cookie) in two browser tabs. Both tabs hit ServeSSE → Subscribe(sessionID) returns the SAME streamEntry (manager.go:114-116). When EITHER tab closes, ServeSSE's `defer m.Unsubscribe(sessionID)` runs, which closes entry.done and deletes the stream from m.streams (manager.go:129-140) with no refcount. The still-open tab's ServeSSE select then fires on `<-entry.done` and returns, killing its live updates; subsequent Push() finds no stream (hasStream=false) and silently drops every update until the surviving tab reloads.
findings.evidence: Subscribe returns the existing entry.ch for a repeat sessionID (manager.go:114-116, asserted by TestManagerSubscribe). Unsubscribe unconditionally `delete(m.streams, sessionID)` + `close(entry.done)` (manager.go:138-139) with no connection counter. stream.go ServeSSE defers m.Unsubscribe(sessionID) (stream.go:46) and exits on `<-entry.done` (stream.go:55). No test covers two concurrent subscribers where one unsubscribes; existing tests only check single-connection teardown and that double-subscribe returns the same channel.
findings.proposedFix: Refcount subscribers per session: increment in Subscribe, decrement in Unsubscribe, and only close(entry.done)+delete when the count reaches zero. Or mint a per-connection streamEntry keyed by a connection nonce rather than sharing one channel per session (which also fixes the competing-consumer fan-out where each update reaches only one of N tabs).

verdict.reason: Verified in current code. Subscribe shares one *streamEntry per sessionID (manager.go:114-116) and Unsubscribe unconditionally deletes m.streams[sessionID] and close(entry.done) with no refcount (manager.go:138-139). ServeSSE does `defer m.Unsubscribe(sessionID)` (stream.go:46) and returns on `<-entry.done` (stream.go:55). Two tabs sharing the same cookie-session both get the same entry; when one closes, the survivor's select fires on entry.done and exits, and subsequent Push finds hasStream=false (manager.go:89,92) so every update is dropped until reload. The real uihost path (uihost.go:1231) validates the session is genuine but reuses the same per-user session across tabs, so the bug is reachable in production. The trigger is a legitimate same-user multi-tab scenario, not attacker-as-config, so this is a correctness/availability defect (P3), not a security boundary bypass. No *_security_test.go asserts the two-subscriber-one-unsubscribes property: TestManagerSubscribe only asserts double-subscribe returns the same channel (the very shared-entry behavior that causes this) and TestManagerUnsubscribe only checks single-connection done-close. The streamEntry doc-comment (manager.go:14-16) only documents never-closing the data channel; it does not sanction first-disconnect teardown, so a refcount fix contradicts no contract.

findings.property: Cached responses for one host/authority must not be served to requests for a different host sharing the same Cache instance.
findings.title: Cache key omits request Host: cross-host content leak
findings.attack: A single binary serves multiple authorities (virtual hosts / multi-tenant subdomains) through one CacheMiddleware+Cache. An anonymous (credential-less) `GET /dashboard` on `tenant-a.app.com` is stored under key `GET:/dashboard?`. An anonymous `GET /dashboard` on `tenant-b.app.com` resolves to the same key and is served tenant-a's cached body (X-Cache=HIT). An attacker can also poison: prime the key via `Host: a` then have victims on `Host: b` read it.
findings.evidence: baseKey at middleware.go:58 is built solely from `r.Method`, `r.URL.Path`, and `r.URL.RawQuery`. For server-side requests `r.URL.Host` is empty and the authority lives in `r.Host`, which is never incorporated into the key. There is no Host/authority component and no `Vary: Host` handling, so distinct authorities collide on identical method+path+query.
findings.proposedFix: Include `r.Host` in baseKey (e.g. `r.Method+":"+r.Host+r.URL.Path+"?"+r.URL.RawQuery`), or document that the middleware is single-authority-only and require callers to scope per-host. Including Host is the safer default.

verdict.reason: Confirmed in current code: middleware.go:58 builds baseKey = r.Method + ":" + r.URL.Path + "?" + r.URL.RawQuery with no r.Host component, and the Vary mechanism (captureVariant/variantMatches) only reads r.Header.Get(name) — r.Host is not a normal request header server-side, so even a Vary: Host response would not scope by authority. With one shared Cache+CacheMiddleware fronting multiple virtual hosts, two anonymous (no Authorization/Cookie, no Set-Cookie) GET /path requests on tenant-a and tenant-b collide on the same key, so tenant-b can be served tenant-a's body (X-Cache=HIT) and the key is poisonable. cache_security_test.go asserts Set-Cookie/private/no-store/no-cache/Vary-*/credentialed/5xx properties but has NO cross-host isolation test, so it is not already covered. No doc comment promises single-authority-only behavior, so a fix would not contradict an escape hatch. Severity P3 is appropriate: it requires the developer to deploy one shared Cache across multiple authorities and only leaks non-credentialed content; single-authority usage (the documented example r.Use(cache.CacheMiddleware(c, ...))) is unaffected.

findings.property: A single-use reset token must only be consumed when it actually results in a password change; consuming it on a request that fails validation strands the user.
findings.title: Reset token consumed before new-password validation/hashing
findings.attack: POST /auth/reset-password with a valid token but an invalid new password (e.g. 7 chars, which fails ValidatePasswordStrength, or a value that trips HashPassword) — the handler calls RedeemToken (single-use delete, magiclink.go:139) at line 181 BEFORE ValidatePasswordStrength at line 194. The token is irrevocably burned, the password is unchanged, and the user gets a 400. The only recovery is to restart the entire forgot-password flow for a new emailed token.
findings.evidence: Order in resetHandler: RedeemToken (line 181) -> PasswordSetter assertion -> ValidatePasswordStrength (line 194) -> HashPassword (line 199) -> SetPassword (line 204). MemoryMagicLinkTokenStore.RedeemToken deletes the entry unconditionally on lookup (magiclink.go:139). So any post-redeem failure (weak password, hash error, store error) leaves the token consumed without a password change. No test in password_reset_security_test.go or verify_reset_test.go asserts the token survives a validation failure.
findings.proposedFix: Validate the new password (ValidatePasswordStrength and the length cap) and confirm the store implements PasswordSetter BEFORE calling RedeemToken, so a bad request returns 400 without burning the token. Only redeem the token once the inputs are known-good and immediately before SetPassword.

verdict.reason: Verified in current code: resetHandler calls p.store.RedeemToken at password_reset.go:181, which (MemoryMagicLinkTokenStore.RedeemToken, magiclink.go:139) unconditionally `delete(m.tokens, token)` before returning, BEFORE ValidatePasswordStrength runs at line 194. ValidatePasswordStrength (password.go:67) rejects passwords < 8 bytes with a 400, so a valid token + a 7-char password burns the single-use token without changing the password, stranding the user (only the >128-byte cap at line 176 is checked pre-redeem; the strength check is post-redeem). No test in password_reset_security_test.go or verify_reset_test.go asserts the token survives a post-redeem validation failure. This is a genuine, triggerable correctness defect but a low-severity UX papercut, not a security vuln: the trigger requires the legitimate user's own valid token plus their own weak password — no auth bypass, escalation, info leak, or cross-user DoS. The finder's bug/P3 classification is accurate; fixing it (validate strength + PasswordSetter assertion before RedeemToken) contradicts no documented contract — the RedeemToken doc-comment describes the store primitive, not a handler ordering guarantee.

findings.property: A Migrator constructed WithDialect(DialectSQLite) must emit DDL and run introspection that the SQLite engine can execute.
findings.title: Diff emits Postgres-only DDL and catalog regardless of dialect
findings.attack: Construct migrate.New(db, WithDialect(DialectSQLite)) against a SQLite DB and call Diff. discoverTables runs SELECT ... FROM information_schema.tables (does not exist in SQLite) so Diff errors out; even if it reached generateCreateTable, the produced CREATE TABLE contains 'id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY', 'JSONB', 'UUID', and 'DEFAULT NOW()' which SQLite rejects.
findings.evidence: discoverTables (diff.go:178-179, 200-202) hardcodes the Postgres information_schema catalog with no dialect branch, unlike runner.go which branches on m.dialect via nowFunc()/placeholder(). generateCreateTable (diff.go:233,259,265) and fieldTypeToSQL (diff.go:41 BIGINT, :47 JSONB, :41-42 UUID) emit only Postgres syntax; the Migrator's m.dialect field is never consulted in diff.go. So WithDialect(DialectSQLite) is silently ineffective for Diff.
findings.proposedFix: Branch discoverTables and the type/DDL generation on m.dialect: use sqlite_master / PRAGMA table_info for SQLite introspection and emit INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT / TEXT / CURRENT_TIMESTAMP for the SQLite dialect, mirroring runner.go's existing dialect handling.

verdict.reason: Verified and reproducible: the package explicitly supports DialectSQLite (migrate.go:26, exercised by TestSQLiteIntegration), and runner.go honors it via nowFunc()/placeholder(), but diff.go never consults m.dialect. discoverTables (diff.go:178-179, 201) hardcodes information_schema, which SQLite lacks, so Diff() errors against a SQLite DB; even past that, generateCreateTable emits BIGSERIAL (diff.go:234) and NOW() (diff.go:259,262) and fieldTypeToSQL emits JSONB/UUID/DOUBLE PRECISION — all Postgres-only. No sibling *_security_test.go exists and migrate_test.go never calls Diff under any dialect (only generateCreateTable directly at line 629, with no SQLite-output assertion), so the property is uncovered. This is a genuine functional correctness bug, but it is developer-config (not attacker input) and has no security impact — P3 is correct. No doc-comment restricts Diff to Postgres; fieldTypeToSQL's "PostgreSQL column type" comment is an implementation note, not a documented escape hatch, so fixing it contradicts nothing.
